Name: effective_resistance_trainability
Title: Effective Resistance as a Structural Predictor and Design Principle for Trainable Deep Networks
Short Hypothesis: The effective resistance (a global graph-theoretic measure of connectivity) of an architecture's residual/shortcut graph between input and output predicts optimization ease, gradient propagation, and perturbation robustness; explicitly reducing effective resistance via a small number of well‑placed shortcuts improves training and robustness more effectively than naively adding residuals or minimizing path length. This setting—modeling a deep architecture as a graph of modules plus shortcut edges—cleanly isolates structural effects on trainability that are not captured by local layerwise or path‑length analyses.
Related Work: Residual networks, highway networks, and dense connections: ResNets (He et al.) and DenseNets explain many empirical gains by easing gradient flow or providing short paths; Veit et al. (2016) interpreted ResNets as ensembles of shallow networks. Mean‑field and dynamical isometry lines of work (Poole, Schoenholz, Pennington et al.) study signal/gradient propagation at initialization and constrain layerwise transforms. Neural architecture search (NAS) and shortcut/topology design explore where to place skips but typically optimize empirically. Graph theory concepts such as effective resistance and electrical network analogies are popular in graph algorithms (sparsification) and some GNN analyses, but to our knowledge they have not been used as an explanatory or prescriptive metric for deep architecture design. This proposal differs from prior work by: (1) treating the architecture as an explicit graph and using effective resistance (a global metric sensitive to number and redundancy of parallel paths) rather than shortest path or local layer metrics; (2) testing whether resistance predicts training dynamics and robustness across model families (CNNs, Transformers); and (3) using resistance as an inexpensive heuristic/regularizer inside NAS and as a guide for low‑cost shortcut placement. This is not a trivial extension of residual work because effective resistance captures global redundancy and interaction among many paths that simple skip counts or path lengths do not.
Abstract: Why do residual connections help train deep networks so broadly across architectures and scales? We propose a novel, simple, and testable structural principle: model the architecture as a graph of computation modules plus shortcut edges, and use effective resistance (the resistance distance from electrical network theory) between input and output as a compact predictor of trainability, gradient propagation, and perturbation robustness. Unlike shortest‑path or local layerwise analyses, effective resistance captures global connectivity and the multiplicity and independence of parallel information pathways. We hypothesize (i) architectures with lower effective resistance are easier to optimize, exhibit more stable gradient flow, and are more robust to weight perturbations; and (ii) targeted reductions in effective resistance via a small number of well placed shortcuts yield larger training and robustness gains per parameter than naively adding residual connections or minimizing only path length. The proposal develops the mapping from architecture to graph, computationally efficient resistance estimates for practical networks, theoretical intuition connecting resistance to gradient variance, and a suite of experiments across CNNs and Transformer stacks. We further show how effective resistance can be used as a lightweight NAS bias and as a shortcut placement heuristic. The project produces actionable design rules for next‑generation architectures and a compact metric for architecture evaluation.
Experiments: 1) Definitions and tools: define nodes as modular blocks (e.g., residual block, transformer layer, conv stage) and edges for feedforward links and shortcuts; compute effective resistance using Laplacian pseudoinverse on the undirected symmetrized graph; implement scalable estimators (Kirchhoff index approximations, random‑walk based estimators) for large stacks. 2) Baseline correlation study: construct families of architectures (plain deep stacks, ResNet variants, DenseNet variants, Transformer encoder stacks) with controlled depth/width; for each architecture measure effective resistance and training metrics (time/steps to reach target training loss/accuracy on CIFAR‑10 and small ImageNet subset, initial gradient norm and layerwise gradient variance, training stability—frequency of exploding/vanishing gradients). Report Spearman/Pearson correlations and partial correlations controlling for depth and parameter count. 3) Randomized shortcuts ablations: for fixed budget of extra shortcut edges, compare three placement strategies: (a) long random skips, (b) nearest neighbor/local skips (like DenseNet), (c) resistance‑guided skips (greedy add edge that most reduces effective resistance). Train each variant on CIFAR‑10 and a small LM task (tiny Transformer on WikiText‑2). Compare convergence speed, final accuracy, gradient statistics, and robustness to Gaussian weight perturbations and adversarial input perturbations. 4) Synthetic signal propagation tests: linear deep networks and two‑layer blocks with known inputs; measure analytic and empirical gradient decay vs effective resistance; test the electrical analogy by injecting currentised gradients and measuring layer voltages. 5) Resistance‑aware NAS / regularization: add a cheap penalty / reward to controller or evolutionary search that biases architectures toward lower resistance for fixed param budget. Compare search efficiency and final architectures vs baseline NAS objectives. 6) Ablations and theory checks: compare effective resistance to simpler metrics (mean shortest path length, count of skips, algebraic connectivity / Fiedler value) to show unique predictive power. Also test directed vs. undirected graph modeling choices. Evaluation metrics: training steps to target, final validation accuracy, gradient norm / variance, top Hessian eigenvalue, loss‑landscape sharpness, correlation coefficients, parameter perturbation robustness, compute cost of estimator. All experiments are feasible on academic compute (single‑machine multi‑GPU) using moderate datasets.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Predictive failure: effective resistance may not explain more than simpler metrics (depth, skip count, or algebraic connectivity). We mitigate this by direct comparisons and by using multiple families and controls. 2) Modeling choices: mapping layers/blocks to graph nodes and symmetrizing directed graphs may lose important directional information. We will test multiple graph encodings and report sensitivity. 3) Computational cost: exact pseudoinverse is expensive for very large graphs; we plan to use scalable estimators (randomized trace/Lanczos, random‑walk approximations) and restrict node granularity to blocks to keep graphs small. 4) Confounding factors: parameter count, width, normalization layers, and initialization schemes may interact with structure; experiments will control for these and include ablations. 5) Non‑universality: resistance may be most useful for architectures built from modular blocks with clear shortcut semantics (ResNet/Transformer stacks) and less applicable to exotic or highly irregular cell‑based NAS designs. 6) Theoretical limits: deriving rigorous bounds connecting effective resistance to optimization rates may be hard; we emphasize empirical evidence and intuitive analytical connections instead.

Name: gradient_frustration
Title: Gradient Frustration: Measuring and Reducing Conflicting Gradient Flows in Residual Architectures
Short Hypothesis: Residual (and shortcut) pathways create multiple, partially-independent routes for gradients to reach parameters; when these routes produce conflicting gradient directions (high 'gradient frustration') optimization slows, becomes unstable, and models are less robust. A simple, local measure of gradient frustration plus lightweight interventions (skip gating and a cheap gradient-alignment regularizer or greedy skip placement) will reduce conflict and improve training speed, stability, and robustness more efficiently than naive shortcut addition or path-length heuristics. This is best explored in modular stacks (ResNets, Transformer blocks) where branches and skips are explicit, allowing clean isolation of branch-wise gradient contributions.
Related Work: Residual networks and skip connections (He et al.) are often justified by improved gradient flow and by an ensemble-of-short-paths perspective (Veit et al.). Other lines study signal/gradient propagation at initialization (mean-field/dynamical isometry). Multi-task and multi-objective optimization work has proposed gradient surgery (PCGrad) and alignment techniques to resolve interfering gradients across tasks. Stochastic depth and other path-drop methods alter path ensembles to improve training. However, prior work has not (a) directly measured signed/angle conflicts among gradients originating from distinct architectural paths inside a single task network, (b) formalized a compact 'frustration' metric that predicts trainability across architectures, nor (c) proposed simple structural or regularization interventions guided by that metric. Our proposal differs by treating residual/skip branches as distinct gradient sources, defining frustration based on pairwise gradient angles and cyclic sign conflicts, and using that to drive architectural and regularization choices rather than treating skips purely as signal shortcuts.
Abstract: Why do residual connections so reliably help optimization across architectures? We hypothesize a complementary explanation: residuals create parallel gradient routes which can sometimes conflict, producing 'gradient frustration' that slows optimization, increases instability, and reduces robustness. We introduce a simple, local/efficient frustration metric that quantifies signed and angular disagreement among gradient contributions from different branches and loops in a modular architecture. We show how to (i) measure frustration cheaply during training using stop-gradient isolations and sub-sampled Jacobian-vector products, (ii) reduce frustration via three practical interventions—learnable skip gates initialized to favor the dominant branch, a lightweight gradient-alignment regularizer that penalizes negative branch-dot-products on a sampled subset of parameters, and a greedy skip-placement heuristic that, for a small budget of extra shortcuts, selects edges that minimize initial frustration—and (iii) evaluate effects across ResNet-like CNNs and Transformer stacks. We demonstrate that frustration predicts optimization difficulty beyond depth and parameter count, that reducing frustration accelerates convergence and improves robustness to weight/input perturbations, and that the interventions give better training-per-parameter gains than naive skip addition. The approach yields actionable diagnostics and low-cost architectural design rules for more trainable deep networks.
Experiments: 1) Frustration definition and measurement: for each residual block with output y = x + F(x), define branch gradients g_skip (gradient through identity path) and g_res (gradient through F) by isolating branches via stop-gradient masks; compute cosine similarity, signed dot products, and a local cyclic frustration score for small cycles (three- and four-node cycles induced by skips). Validate that these measures are stable across minibatches and cheaply estimated by sub-sampling parameters. 2) Correlation study: construct families of architectures (plain deep stacks, ResNet variants with varied skip density and scaled skips, Transformer encoder stacks with/without pre-LN) controlling for depth and parameter count; measure initial and early-training frustration and correlate with optimization speed (steps to reach target loss), instability events (exploding/vanishing gradients), and final test accuracy on CIFAR-10/100 and Tiny-ImageNet; report Spearman correlations and partial correlations. 3) Interventions ablation: implement three interventions—(A) learnable skip gates (single scalar per block) with small gradient-alignment bias during initialization; (B) gradient-alignment regularizer R = lambda * E_s[ ReLU(-cos(g_a,g_b)) ] computed on a sampled subset s of parameters/blocks to keep cost low; (C) greedy skip-placement: for a fixed extra-skip budget, greedily add the skip that maximally reduces initial frustration. Train each variant vs. baselines (vanilla ResNet, scaled residuals, stochastic depth) and measure training speed, final accuracy, gradient norms, gradient cosine distributions, top Hessian eigenvalue, and robustness to random weight perturbations and common corruptions (CIFAR-C). 4) Small Transformer study: apply the same measurements and regularizers to transformer encoder stacks (attention block + residual) on WikiText-2 / PTB language modeling and GLUE-style small tasks to test cross-family generality. 5) Ablations and comparisons: compare frustration to simpler predictors (skip count, mean shortest path, algebraic connectivity, initial gradient norm) to show unique predictive power. Implementation notes: isolate branch gradients by masking or scaling branches to 0/1 during an extra backward pass; compute regularizer on small parameter subsets to keep overhead ~10-20% of training time. Evaluation metrics: steps-to-target, final accuracy/perplexity, Spearman correlations, mean cosine similarity, frequency of negative dot products, robustness measures, compute overhead.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Measurement noise and cost: isolating branch gradients requires extra backward work; although we propose subsampling and stop-gradient tricks, overhead may be nontrivial. Mitigation: use small parameter subsets and infrequent measurement (once per N steps). 2) No causal effect: frustration may correlate with poor training but not be causal; our interventions test causality but may have modest effect. 3) Regularizer harms diversity: aligning gradients might suppress beneficial diversity between branches, reducing expressivity; we will tune regularizer strength and only align on small parameter subsets. 4) Applicability: technique relies on clear branch structure; it is less applicable to monolithic cells or architectures without explicit shortcuts. 5) Theoretical analysis: deriving tight optimization-theory links between frustration and convergence may be hard; this work focuses on empirical diagnostics and practical fixes rather than full theory. 6) Overfitting intervention: gate parameters or alignment loss could overfit small datasets; cross-validate and use appropriate regularization.

Name: gramian_trainability
Title: Controllable and Observable: Gramian-Guided Design for Trainable Deep Networks
Short Hypothesis: Local linear controllability and observability Gramians (and their Hankel singular spectra) computed at initialization predict a deep architecture's trainability, gradient stability, and perturbation robustness across CNN and Transformer families; moreover, small, targeted structural interventions (adding/biasing skips or light bypasses) that increase dominant Hankel singular values or balance controllability vs. observability improve optimization speed and robustness more efficiently than generic skip addition. Modeling blocks as states and using randomized Jacobian-vector products yields scalable estimators, enabling Gramian-aware architecture design and lightweight regularizers for practical networks.
Related Work: Residual networks, highway nets and DenseNets explain empirical gains via shortcut paths and improved gradient flow. Mean-field and dynamical-isometry works (Poole, Schoenholz, Pennington et al.) study signal/gradient propagation at initialization. Recent work (Moon 2025) used local linearization and Gramians to interpret trained networks. Our proposal differs in three ways: (1) we use controllability/observability Gramians and Hankel singular values as predictive, actionable metrics computed at initialization (not solely post hoc interpretability); (2) we develop scalable randomized estimators and algorithms that alter architecture (skip placement, tiny bypass modules, Gramian regularizers) to directly increase Gramian-derived trainability measures under tight parameter budgets; (3) we provide causal tests (interventions) across CNNs and Transformers showing that Gramian-aware changes accelerate convergence and improve robustness, distinguishing this from prior analytic or purely interpretive usage of Gramians.
Abstract: Why do residual-like structures make deep networks so trainable? We propose a control-theoretic structural principle: treat hidden block activations as a dynamical state and use local linear controllability and observability Gramians (and induced Hankel singular values) computed at initialization as compact predictors of optimization ease, gradient stability, and perturbation robustness. Dominant Hankel values capture how easily inputs can excite internal modes that influence outputs — an architecture lacking controllable or observable modes will be hard to optimize or brittle. We introduce scalable estimators based on Jacobian-vector products, randomized projections, and block-level state modeling that make Gramian computation tractable for modern networks. Using these estimators we (i) show strong correlations between initial Gramian/Hankel spectra and training speed/robustness across ResNet variants, plain stacks, Dense-like dense-skip patterns, and Transformer encoders; (ii) propose cheap structural interventions — Gramian-guided skip/bypass placement and a Gramian-regularizer that rewards larger trace/log-det of controllability/observability under a parameter budget — and demonstrate causal improvements in convergence and robustness; (iii) compare Gramian metrics to simpler predictors (path length, skip count, effective resistance, gradient-frustration) and show superior, complementary predictive power. The work yields practical diagnostics and prescriptive design rules for building more trainable, robust deep architectures.
Experiments: 1) Definitions & Scalable Estimators: model network as sequence of B block-states s_1..s_B (e.g., residual block outputs, transformer layer outputs). Linearize f around input x0: compute Jacobians J_x->s and J_s->y via efficient Jacobian-vector products (autodiff) on a small input batch. Estimate controllability Gramian W_c ≈ sum_k J_x->s J_x->s^T (energy from input directions) and observability Gramian W_o ≈ sum_k J_s->y^T J_s->y via randomized trace/Lanczos to get leading singular values / Hankel spectrum. Validate estimator error vs exact for small models. 2) Correlation study: across families (plain deep MLPs, ResNet variants, DenseNet-like, Transformer encoder stacks) and controlled budgets (depth, params), compute initial Gramian/Hankel stats (dominant singular values, trace ratio W_c/W_o, Hankel decay) and correlate (Spearman) with trainability metrics: steps-to-target loss/accuracy (CIFAR-10/100, TinyImageNet, WikiText-2 lm), early gradient norm stability, frequency of instability events, top Hessian eigenvalue, and robustness to random weight perturbations and CIFAR-C corruptions. 3) Causal interventions — Gramian-guided skips: with a fixed extra-parameter budget, compare (A) random skip insertion, (B) longest skips, (C) Gramian-greedy skip insertion (choose skip that maximally increases dominant Hankel value estimated cheaply). Train each and compare convergence speed, final accuracy, robustness. 4) Gramian-regularizer & light bypass modules: two algorithmic interventions — (i) add tiny learnable linear bypasses scaled by factors chosen to maximize trace(W_c) or log-det(W_c+eps I) during early training (implemented as extra 1x1 conv or linear projection with small param count), (ii) add a loss term L_g = -lambda * trace_est(W_c) (or -lambda * sum top-k Hankel values) computed on mini-batches and applied only early in training. Compare to baseline skip-scaling, stochastic depth, and weight normalization. 5) Transformer study: apply identical pipeline to Transformer encoder stacks (attention + FFN) on small LM tasks; examine whether pre-LN vs post-LN affects Gramians and trainability; test Gramian-guided attention residual gating. 6) Baselines and ablations: compare predictiveness and intervention gains against effective resistance, gradient-frustration, mean shortest path, and algebraic connectivity. Metrics: Spearman/Pearson correlations, AUC of predictive classifier for 'easy' vs 'hard' architectures, steps-to-target, final accuracy/perplexity, robustness metrics, compute overhead of estimators. All experiments use block-level modeling and randomized estimators to fit on single-machine multi-GPU academic budgets.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Linearization gap: Gramians rely on local linearization which may miss global nonlinear effects; we mitigate via sampling multiple input operating points and focusing on early-training predictive power. 2) Computational cost: Jacobian-based estimates can be expensive; we use block-level states, randomized projections, and infrequent evaluations. 3) Non-predictive outcome: Gramians might correlate weakly with trainability in some architectures; we will directly compare to other predictors and report negative results. 4) Intervention fragility: Adding bypasses or regularizers to boost Gramians might harm expressivity or overfit; we constrain param budgets and only apply early/weak regularization. 5) Hyperparameter sensitivity: Gramian regularizer strength, frequency of estimation, and block granularity introduce tuning; report sensitivity analyses and provide default, low-cost settings. 6) Applicability scope: best suited for modular stacks with clear block states (ResNets, Transformers); extensions to highly irregular NAS cells may require redesigning state definitions. 7) Theoretical limits: proving tight convergence bounds from Gramian spectra is challenging; the work focuses on empirical predictivity and practical design heuristics rather than full rigorous guarantees.

Name: modular_smallworld_rewiring
Title: Modular Small-World Scaffolds and Early Rewiring for Trainable Deep Networks
Short Hypothesis: Deep architectures whose residual/shortcut graph is organized as a hierarchical, modular small-world scaffold—highly clustered local modules plus a sparse set of strategic long-range shortcuts—are more trainable, robust, and parameter-efficient than plain, dense-skip, or naïvely small-world designs. Further, a lightweight, activity-driven early-rewiring procedure that adds (or gates-on) a few inter-module shortcuts during initial epochs, based on measured activation/gradient correlations, yields most of the gains with minimal overhead. This setting (block-level modules, gated skips, early rewiring) isolates structural effects from parametric changes and is the right setting because it mirrors the modular/connector-hub organization found useful in biological and engineered networks while remaining cheap to implement in standard deep models.
Related Work: Small-world-inspired architectures (e.g., SWANN 2021) show faster convergence by introducing long-range cross-layer links. Residual networks, DenseNets, and other shortcut designs improve optimization by providing short paths. Dynamic sparse training and rewiring methods (RigL, SET) alter weight-level connectivity during training, often guided by weight magnitude. Brain and network-science literature emphasizes modularity plus a few connector hubs as an efficient trade-off between segregation and integration. Our proposal differs in three key ways: (1) structural granularity: we operate at the block/module level (residual blocks, transformer layers) to control topology without changing parameterization per se; (2) topology: we focus on hierarchical modular small-world scaffolds (tunable modularity + few long-range connector shortcuts) rather than flat small-world rewiring or dense skipping; (3) adaptive wiring: we introduce a cheap, activity-driven early-rewiring algorithm that selects a tiny budget of inter-module shortcuts based on activation/gradient similarity measured in the first few epochs, rather than pruning/rewiring based on weight magnitudes. These aspects are not trivial extensions of prior work and offer new, testable design principles for trainable networks.
Abstract: Why do residual and shortcut connections help so reliably? Prior small-world and dense-skip approaches add long-range links or many shortcuts, but overlook an important structural trade-off: local specialization (modularity) vs global integration (long-range connectors). We propose a simple, biologically inspired design principle: organize the computational graph of a deep model into hierarchical modules with dense local skips and a sparse set of strategic long-range connector skips to achieve a modular small-world scaffold. We further propose an inexpensive early-rewiring procedure that, during the first few epochs, measures inter-module activation/gradient correlations on small batches and gates on a tiny set of shortcuts that most increase integration while preserving modular clustering. Our hypotheses are (i) modular small-world scaffolds give better gradient propagation, spectral properties (favorable algebraic connectivity vs clustering), and robustness per parameter than plain ResNets, DenseNets, or random small-world topologies; and (ii) most benefits can be obtained by adding only a few activity-chosen long-range shortcuts early in training via learnable gates, avoiding large parameter overhead or unstable late rewiring. We provide scalable construction rules, a low-cost rewiring algorithm, and a suite of experiments on CNN and Transformer families showing improved convergence speed, final accuracy, and perturbation robustness, along with analyses linking modularity, clustering, and spectral measures to trainability. The approach yields actionable, low-cost architectural guidelines and a small set of practical mechanisms for next-generation trainable networks.
Experiments: 1) Scaffold construction and metrics: define block-level nodes (residual blocks, transformer layers) and generate families of topologies with controlled parameters: number of modules M, intra-module skip density p_local, long-range edge budget B. Measure graph metrics: clustering coefficient C, mean shortest path L, modularity Q (Louvain), small-worldness sigma, algebraic connectivity (Fiedler value). 2) Baseline comparison: implement modular small-world scaffolds in ResNet-like CNNs and Transformer encoder stacks (block-level scaffolds) and compare to baselines: plain deep stack (no skips), standard ResNet (identity skips), DenseNet-like dense local skips, SWANN-style random small-world (flat rewiring), and random-skip baselines. Keep parameter count close by using gated 1x1 bypass projections for added shortcuts or sharing tiny projection modules; evaluate on CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, and WikiText-2 (small LM). Metrics: steps-to-target loss/accuracy, final validation accuracy/perplexity, per-parameter training efficiency, robustness to Gaussian weight perturbations and CIFAR-C corruptions, gradient norm stability, top Hessian eigenvalue, and compute overhead. 3) Early activity-driven rewiring algorithm: during initial T_e epochs (e.g., first 5 epochs), sample small batches and compute cheap inter-block affinity scores using (A) activation cross-correlations (after global pooling) and (B) cosine similarity of block-output gradients estimated via one backward pass. For a fixed small budget B (e.g., 2-10 shortcuts), greedily add or gate-on the edges with highest affinities connecting different modules. Implement new shortcuts as gated bypasses with small 1x1 conv/linear projection scaled by gate initialized to zero, then unfreeze gates. Compare to random-add and magnitude-based-add baselines. 4) Ablations: vary M, p_local, B to map the design trade-space; ablate whether shortcuts connect module hubs (highest centrality) or random nodes; test whether early-only rewiring (fixed thereafter) outperforms continual rewiring. 5) Spectral and optimization analysis: correlate graph metrics (Q, C, L, sigma, Fiedler) with trainability measures; measure how added long-range edges affect gradient variance across blocks and condition number of block-Jacobian approximations. 6) Transformer study: group layers into stages (e.g., 4-stage groups) and apply scaffold+rewiring to add cross-stage residuals; test on small GLUE tasks and LM to demonstrate cross-family generality. Implementation details: compute affinities on tiny batches to keep overhead <10% and perform rewiring infrequently. All experiments fit academic compute (single multi-GPU machine).
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Prior art overlap: SWANN and some small-world proposals already showed benefits from long-range links; we mitigate overlap by emphasizing hierarchical modularity plus activity-driven early rewiring at block granularity and providing detailed baselines to demonstrate unique gains. 2) Marginal gains: benefits may be small or dataset-dependent; we will map the design space and report when and why gains arise. 3) Rewiring instability: adding shortcuts can destabilize training if applied naively; we use gated bypasses initialized closed (zero) and unfreeze slowly, and restrict rewiring to early training. 4) Measurement cost: computing inter-block affinities adds overhead; we limit to tiny batches and rare evaluations and show sensitivity to estimation frequency. 5) Applicability: methods assume a meaningful module decomposition (stacks/stages); highly irregular NAS cells may need adaptation. 6) Hyperparameter tuning: choices (module count, budget B, rewiring window T_e) require tuning; the paper will provide presets and sensitivity analyses. 7) Theoretical explanation: providing rigorous convergence proofs linking modular small-world metrics to optimization rates is hard; we focus on empirical evidence and mechanistic analyses (spectral, gradient statistics).

Name: architectural_eigenmode_alignment
Title: Architectural Eigenmode Alignment: Matching Network Propagation Modes to Data Spectra for Trainable Deep Networks
Short Hypothesis: Trainability and early optimization of deep architectures depend strongly on how well an architecture's intrinsic linear propagation modes (the dominant singular/eigenmodes of its block-level linearization or residual-path graph) align with the dominant eigenmodes of the data distribution. Simple, low-cost structural interventions that increase this alignment at initialization (spectral bypasses, PCA-initialized low-rank skips, or learnable spectral gates) speed convergence, stabilize gradients, and improve robustness with minimal parameter overhead. This hypothesis isolates a structural — data-dependent spectral — factor that is not captured by path-length, skip-count, or mean-field signal propagation analyses, and it is best tested in modular stacks (ResNets, Transformers, MLP stages) where block-level linearizations and data covariances are cheaply estimable.
Related Work: Relevant strands include signal/gradient propagation and dynamical isometry (Poole, Schoenholz, Pennington), data-dependent initializations and orthogonal initializations (Saxe et al. line), NTK/Jacobian-spectrum analyses relating singular values to training dynamics, and work on low-rank adapters and bypass modules for efficient adaptation. There is also literature on architecture topology (shortcuts, DenseNets) and on control/Gramian perspectives on trainability. However, prior work focuses on global singular-value distributions, path-length arguments, or layerwise isometry, and typically treats data only indirectly (through input variances). Our proposal is distinct in three ways: (1) it centers on alignment between architecture-intrinsic propagation eigenmodes (block Jacobian or residual-graph eigenvectors) and empirical data principal directions as a predictor of trainability; (2) it proposes concrete, low-cost structural interventions (spectral bypasses, PCA-initialized low-rank skips, and learnable spectral gates) that explicitly raise alignment at initialization under tight parameter budgets; (3) it develops scalable randomized estimators to measure alignment in large models and performs causal intervention studies across CNNs and Transformer stacks. This is not a trivial combination of prior ideas because it ties structural design decisions directly to the data spectrum and provides prescriptive, parameter-efficient changes rather than only initialization heuristics.
Abstract: Residual and shortcut connections dramatically improve deep learning optimization, but why certain designs work so broadly remains open. We propose a new, data-aware structural principle: the early-trainability of a modular deep network depends on the alignment between the architecture's linear propagation eigenmodes (dominant singular/eigenvectors of block-level Jacobians or the residual-graph Laplacian) and the dominant eigenmodes of the data covariance. Intuitively, if an architecture naturally propagates and amplifies the subspaces that the data uses most, gradient signals and representation learning start productive immediately; misaligned architectures waste early updates adjusting basis rotations, slowing convergence and risking instability. We introduce scalable estimators for block-level propagation modes and for data principal components via randomized SVD and Jacobian-vector products, define compact alignment metrics (subspace angles, CCA), and propose three lightweight, testable interventions: (i) PCA-initialized low-rank bypasses (tiny projection skips initialized to map data PCs directly into later blocks); (ii) spectral gates—per-block diagonal spectral scaling matrices initialized to favor data PCs; (iii) randomized spectral regularizers applied only in early epochs to bias gradients toward aligned modes. We will correlate alignment with trainability across ResNet, Transformer, and MLP families, and show causal gains from the interventions on CIFAR/Tiny-ImageNet and small language-modeling tasks. The outcome is a practical, data-dependent structural design principle and inexpensive mechanisms to make deep networks more immediately trainable and robust.
Experiments: 1) Measurement pipeline and sanity checks: define block-states as outputs after each residual block or transformer layer. For a small held batch, estimate (A) data principal components (top-k PCs of activation/input covariance) via randomized SVD, and (B) block propagation modes: leading right-singular vectors of J_input->block or leading eigenvectors of symmetrized block-graph Laplacian approximations via randomized Lanczos on Jacobian-vector products. Compute alignment metrics: principal angles, subspace CCA canonical correlations, and fraction of propagated energy in data PC subspace. Validate estimators vs exact on small models to calibrate k and sampling. 2) Correlation study: construct controlled families (plain deep stacks, ResNet variants with varying skip density and scaling, Transformer encoder stacks) matched for depth/params. For each, measure initial alignment and record trainability metrics (steps to reach target training loss, early loss descent slope, gradient-norm stability, final validation accuracy) on CIFAR-10/100 and a small ImageNet subset or Tiny-ImageNet; report Spearman/Pearson correlations and partial correlations controlling for depth/param count. 3) Causal interventions: implement three low-cost mechanisms (A) PCA-initialized low-rank bypasses: add small d x r projection skips (r<<d) where rows map top-r data PCs into later-block inputs, initialized to the PCA directions and scaled small; (B) spectral gates: per-block learnable diagonal gating in an orthonormal basis, initialized to amplify data-PC coordinates; (C) early spectral regularizer: for first E epochs add loss term encouraging gradient energy on top-k PCs. For a fixed small parameter budget, compare intervention vs naive skip addition and vs tuned initialization (orthogonal/PRELU scalings). Measure convergence speed, final accuracy, gradient statistics (cosine with data PCs), top Hessian eigenvalue and robustness to random weight perturbations and CIFAR-C corruptions. 4) Cross-family: apply to Transformer encoders on WikiText-2 / PTB small LM (grouping token embeddings and layer outputs for data PCs) and to simple MLPs on synthetic anisotropic gaussian tasks to test mechanism under controlled spectral gaps. 5) Ablations and comparisons: vary rank r, strength of initialization scaling, and whether PCs are computed on raw inputs vs early-layer activations; compare predictive power of alignment against effective resistance, Gramian/Hankel measures, and gradient-frustration metrics from prior proposals. 6) Efficiency study: measure extra compute and parameters; show that low-rank bypasses (e.g., r=8) give large gains per added parameter. Evaluation metrics: steps-to-target, early-loss slope, Spearman correlation coefficients, canonical correlations, robustness metrics, and training overhead. All experiments use randomized estimators and block granularity so they fit on academic multi-GPU setups.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Local linearization limits: alignment uses linearized propagation around initialization; deep nonlinear dynamics may rapidly change modes making initial alignment less relevant. Mitigation: measure alignment at multiple early checkpoints and focus on early-training dynamics where initialization matters. 2) Data dependence and overfitting: designing architecture to match training-data PCs risks overfitting to dataset idiosyncrasies; mitigate by computing PCs on small held unlabeled subsets, using small ranks r, and applying alignment bias only during early training. 3) Computational cost: PCA and Jacobian-mode estimations add cost; we use randomized SVD and few-vector Jacobian-vector products with small k to keep overhead small and demonstrate favorable parameter-efficiency trade-offs. 4) Expressivity trade-offs: forcing early alignment might reduce needed basis diversity and hurt final performance on some tasks; evaluate with ablations that relax alignment over time (anneal gates or unfreeze bypasses). 5) Limited scope: method best applies when meaningful data PCs exist (anisotropic data) and when models have modular block structure; for highly isotropic or adversarial distributions alignment may be less helpful. 6) Negative results risk: alignment may correlate but not causally improve training; our interventions are designed to test causality. If unsuccessful, we will report negative findings and analyze when and why alignment matters.

Name: structural_motifs_trainability
Title: Motifs Matter: A Motif-Level Science for Trainable Deep Architectures
Short Hypothesis: Small recurring subgraph motifs in an architecture's block-level computational graph (e.g., feedforward chain, bypass triangle, parallel-fork) systematically influence gradient propagation, loss-landscape geometry, and robustness. Motif counts and local motif arrangements predict trainability beyond simple global metrics (depth, skip-count, path length), and small, targeted motif-level interventions (gating, low-cost rewiring to create/remove specific motifs) yield larger per-parameter gains in optimization speed and stability than naïve skip-addition or random rewiring. Studying modular block graphs (ResNet/Transformer stacks and NAS cells) isolates structural effects while remaining experimentally feasible.
Related Work: Network motifs are a well-established concept in network science (Milo et al.), and recent work has begun to analyze motif distributions in trained sparse DNNs (Zahn et al., 2024). Standard deep-learning explanations for residuals emphasize short paths, dynamical isometry, or ensemble interpretations (He et al., Veit et al., Poole et al.). Small-world and rewiring proposals (SWANN et al.) show benefits from added long-range links. However, no prior work has (1) systematically map motif types in block-level computational graphs to trainability metrics across architecture families, (2) provide causal motif-level interventions (add/remove/gate motifs) under tight parameter budgets, nor (3) develop inexpensive motif-aware NAS/regularizers. This proposal differs by treating motifs as mechanistic micro-primitives for optimization (not just descriptive statistics) and by giving prescriptive, low-cost motif surgery and motif-regularized search methods that are experimentally testable on standard benchmarks.
Abstract: Residuals and shortcuts operate at the level of local connectivity patterns; we propose that the explanatory and prescriptive power for trainability lies not only in global counts of skips or path lengths but in the inventory and arrangement of small structural motifs. We introduce a motif-level research program: (i) define a compact motif alphabet for block-level neural graphs (directed 3- and 4-node motifs that capture forks, merges, bypass triangles, parallel pathways, and small feedback-like loops induced by residuals and normalization blocks); (ii) measure motif statistics efficiently and show they predict early optimization dynamics, gradient alignment, and robustness across ResNet variants, Transformer stacks, and common NAS cell designs; (iii) test causal interventions that add or remove specific motif instances via gated bypasses or tiny projection modules under strict parameter budgets; (iv) incorporate motif-aware priors into lightweight NAS controllers and regularizers to bias search toward beneficial motif patterns. We will provide mechanistic analyses (linearized networks, motif transfer functions) linking motifs to gradient variance and Hessian spectra. The result is an actionable motif-level toolbox for designing and diagnosing trainable deep networks that complements path-based and spectral perspectives.
Experiments: 1) Motif alphabet & tooling: define directed motifs at block granularity (examples: chain A->B->C, fork A->{B,C}, merge {A,B}->C, bypass triangle A->B, A->C, B->C (skip), 4-node ladder, small cycle-like patterns deployed by layernorm/skip combos). Implement a fast motif counter for small directed graphs (B up to ~50 blocks) and a randomized motif-sampler for larger stacks. 2) Correlation study across families: construct controlled architecture suites (plain stacks, ResNet variants with varied skip densities and scales, Dense-like local skips, Transformer encoder stacks, representative NAS cells). For each, compute motif inventories (absolute counts and normalized motif frequencies, local motif neighborhoods), and measure trainability metrics: steps-to-target loss/accuracy (CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, WikiText-2 LM), early-loss slope, gradient-norm variance across blocks, frequency of instability events, top Hessian eigenvalue, and robustness to small random weight perturbations and CIFAR-C. Use Spearman/Pearson and partial correlations controlling for depth/params; show motifs add predictive power beyond degree/path metrics. 3) Causal motif surgery: for a fixed tiny parameter budget (e.g., adding gates or 1x1 projections totalling <1-3% params), implement three interventions: (A) create bypass-triangles at targeted layers (add gated skip A->C forming triangle) vs random skip; (B) close harmful motifs (remove/gate off redundant parallel forks) via learnable gates initialized closed; (C) replace a 3-node problematic motif with an alternative motif (e.g., convert fork+merge pair into a serial chain using small projection). Compare convergence speed, final accuracy, gradient statistics, Hessian top eigenvalue, and robustness. 4) Mechanistic analysis with simplified linear models: design synthetic linear networks whose block-graph motifs are identical but differ in motif counts and analytically study gradient variance propagation and Hessian spectra; verify predictions empirically. 5) Motif-aware NAS & regularizer: implement a simple cell-level motif penalty or reward (e.g., reward count of beneficial motifs per param budget) in a small NAS controller (RL or evolutionary with low-budget search) and compare found cells to baseline NAS under equal budgets. 6) Ablations: compare directed vs symmetrized motifs, motif neighborhood statistics vs global counts, and motif predictive power versus earlier metrics (effective resistance, Gramian/Hankel, gradient-frustration). Report compute cost of motif counting and provide recommended block granularity. All experiments are designed to run on academic multi-GPU hardware using standard datasets and modest search budgets.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Granularity & modeling choices: mapping layers to nodes and choosing motif scale may affect conclusions; we will evaluate sensitivity to node definitions (block vs sub-block) and use multiple granularities. 2) Correlation vs causation: motifs may correlate with other structural confounders (depth, degree); causal motif surgery experiments are included to test interventions, but effects may be modest. 3) Counting cost: exact motif counting scales combinatorially; we mitigate by restricting to small block graphs and randomized sampling/estimators for larger models. 4) Transferability: motif effects found on CNNs/Transformers may not generalize to all architectures (e.g., highly irregular NAS cells); we will characterize domains of applicability. 5) Negative or redundant findings: motif statistics might not offer more predictive signal than simpler metrics; the project will document negative results and analyze when motifs do matter. 6) Theoretical limits: rigorous bounds linking motif counts to optimization rates may be hard; emphasis is on empirical regularities and mechanistic linearized analyses rather than full proofs.

Name: topological_bottlenecks
Title: Topological Bottlenecks: Persistent Homology as a Structural Lens and Design Tool for Trainable Deep Networks
Short Hypothesis: Trainability, early optimization speed, and robustness of deep networks are strongly related to how the topology of intermediate representations (as captured by persistent homology) evolves across layers. Architectures that induce abrupt, large topological changes (topological bottlenecks) early in training are harder to optimize. Measuring inter-layer topology-change with efficient persistent-homology summaries predicts optimization difficulty across CNNs and Transformers, and small, cheap interventions (topology-aware skip gating and an early topological regularizer) that smooth topology evolution greatly improve convergence and robustness per added parameter compared to naive skip additions. This setting—block-level activations, mini-batch PH computations, and gated residuals—isolates structural/topological effects rather than low-level parameterization choices and is the right testbed to study topology-driven trainability.
Related Work: Topological data analysis (TDA) and persistent homology (PH) have been applied to feature augmentation, shape recognition, and a few specialized topology-aware losses. Recent works use PH for point-cloud networks, LiDAR descriptors, and occasional task-specific topological losses for robustness. However, prior uses treat PH as an auxiliary representation or task-dependent regularizer; there is little systematic study of how representation topology across layers relates to optimization or how to use topology to guide architectural design. A few papers add topological losses to specific tasks (e.g., a PH-based loss for ResNet in an acoustics application), but none develop: (1) inter-layer topology-change as a predictor of trainability across model families (CNNs, Transformers); (2) cheap, scalable PH estimators tailored for block-level analysis in deep stacks; or (3) topology-aware architectural interventions (gated residuals and early topological curricula) evaluated for causal impact on convergence and robustness. Our proposal fills this gap by turning PH into a structural diagnostic and low-cost design tool for trainability, distinct from prior PH-in-feature-pipelines or task-specific topological constraints.
Abstract: Residual connections and other architectural patterns improve optimization, but available explanations (short paths, gradient propagation, spectral properties) leave open structural questions about the shape of internal representations. We propose a simple, practical, and novel structural principle: view a deep network as a pipeline of evolving data manifolds and use persistent homology to measure how representation topology changes across layers. We hypothesize that abrupt, large inter-layer topology changes—topological bottlenecks—indicate representational folding/unfolding that impedes gradient-based optimization, while smoother topological evolution facilitates learning. To test this, we develop scalable PH estimators for block-level activations (random-projection/PCA compression, witness complexes, persistence silhouettes/landscapes) and define compact diagnostics: total persistence, Betti summaries, and an inter-layer Topological Change Score (TCS) based on Wasserstein/bottleneck distances or L2 distances between persistence landscapes. We validate that TCS at initialization and in early training predicts steps-to-target, instability events, and robustness across ResNet-like CNNs, plain stacks, and Transformer encoder stacks. We then introduce two lightweight interventions: (i) topology-aware skip gates—per-skip scalars regularized to reduce early TCS; and (ii) an early topological regularizer applied only during the initial epochs that penalizes excessive layer-to-layer topology jumps (implemented via differentiable persistence summaries). These causeably reduce TCS, accelerate convergence, and improve perturbation robustness with minimal parameter/compute overhead. The project yields a new topological diagnostic and inexpensive design rules for building more trainable deep networks.
Experiments: 1) Tooling & practical PH pipeline: (a) Define block-level activations (outputs after residual block or transformer layer). (b) For each block, collect a small mini-batch of activations (e.g., pooled feature vectors across spatial dims or token embeddings). Compress to low dimension via randomized projection or PCA (k=16-64) to make PH tractable. (c) Compute persistent diagrams using a witness or Vietoris-Rips filtration (Ripser/giotto-tda) and vectorize via persistence landscapes or silhouettes to obtain fixed-length differentiable summaries. (d) Define per-layer summaries: total persistence (sum lifetimes), Betti counts at chosen scales, and inter-layer distance D_i = ||landscape_i - landscape_{i-1}||_2. Define Topological Change Score (TCS) = sum_i D_i or max_i D_i for the early layers. Validate estimator stability vs batch size and compression k on small models. 2) Observational study: construct controlled families of architectures (plain deep stacks, ResNet variants with varied skip densities and skip scalings, Transformer encoder stacks with/without residual scaling or pre/post-LN). For each architecture compute initial and early-training TCS and correlate (Spearman/Pearson, partial correlations controlling for depth/params) with trainability measures: steps-to-target loss/accuracy on CIFAR-10/100 and Tiny-ImageNet, early-loss slope, frequency of exploding/vanishing gradients, layerwise gradient variance, and robustness to Gaussian weight perturbations and CIFAR-C corruptions. Visualize typical persistence-diagram trajectories across layers for easy vs hard nets. 3) Causal interventions: (A) Topology-aware skip gates: add a scalar gate per residual skip (initialized to 1 or small value) with L2 and topological penalties; early-training objective includes lambda * sum_i D_i estimated on a held mini-batch, encouraging gate values that smooth topology changes. (B) Early topological regularizer: apply L_topo = lambda * sum_layer D_i only for epochs E_0 (e.g., first 5-10 epochs), then remove. Compare to baselines: standard skip-scaling, learning-rate warmup, stochastic depth, and Gramian/ resistance-based interventions. Evaluate convergence speed, final accuracy, gradient statistics, top Hessian eigenvalue, and robustness metrics (weight-noise, common corruptions). 4) Ablations & sensitivity: vary compression dimension k, batch sample size, choice of filtration (Rips vs witness), and persistence-vectorization (landscape vs silhouette). Test directed vs symmetrized activations (for transformers). Examine whether penalizing only max_i D_i (largest jump) or total TCS works better. 5) Cross-family generality: replicate key experiments on Transformer encoders for small LM tasks (WikiText-2) and on small MLPs with synthetic anisotropic manifolds (controlled known topology) to validate mechanism under known ground truth. 6) Comparisons: directly compare predictive power of TCS vs other structural predictors from prior proposals (effective resistance, Gramian/Hankel, gradient-frustration, motif counts) using regression/AUC analyses. All experiments use block granularity, small batches, and randomized PH compressions to be feasible on academic multi-GPU hardware.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Computational cost & noise: PH on high-dimensional activations is costly and noisy; mitigations include PCA/random projections, witness complexes, small batch sizes, and vectorized persistence summaries. Estimators may still add overhead—but we restrict measurement frequency (once per N steps) and early-only evaluation. 2) Sensitivity to design choices: TCS depends on pooling, compression dimension, and filtration; we will provide sensitivity studies and robust defaults. 3) Correlation vs causation: topology may correlate with other confounders (normalization, initialization); causal interventions (gated skips and early regularizer) are included to test direct effects. 4) Over-regularization: penalizing topology change might hurt expressivity or slow later fine-tuning; we apply the regularizer only early and anneal/unfreeze to avoid permanent suppression. 5) Limited scope: method best applies to modular stacks with clear block outputs; highly irregular micro-NAS cells may need adapted node definitions. 6) Theoretical limits: linking PH metrics to provable optimization bounds is challenging; this project emphasizes empirical predictivity, mechanistic linearized intuition, and practical design heuristics rather than full formal guarantees.

Name: intrinsic_timescales
Title: Intrinsic Timescales: Designing Multi-Scale Temporal Modes for Trainable Deep Networks
Short Hypothesis: Beyond path length and mean singular-value targets, the distribution of intrinsic timescales (effective response rates of block-level linearized propagation modes) across a deep architecture shapes early learning dynamics, stability, and robustness. Architectures whose block-wise propagation spectra intentionally span a controlled range of fast-to-slow modes (a multi-timescale scaffold) enable efficient hierarchical credit assignment and smoother loss descent. Low-cost, block-level interventions that create or bias a broad, structured timescale spectrum at initialization (timescale scaffolds, diagonal spectral gates, or alternating fast/slow blocks) will improve optimization speed and perturbation robustness more per parameter than unstructured skip additions or uniform dynamical-isometry approaches.
Related Work: Classic accounts of residual benefits emphasize short paths and avoiding exploding/vanishing gradients; dynamical-isometry and mean-field lines (Saxe, Poole, Pennington et al.) study singular-value distributions at initialization to promote stable signal/gradient propagation. Layer-scaling or skip-scaling methods (e.g., LayerScale) apply uniform block-wise scaling to stabilize training. State-space models (S4) and slow-feature-analysis highlight the value of multi-timescale dynamics in sequence modeling. However, prior work focuses on making singular-value spectra uniform or on single-model families; little attention has been paid to deliberately engineering a heterogeneous, controlled spectrum of intrinsic timescales across blocks as a structural design principle. This proposal differs by (1) defining operational measures of block-level timescales across CNNs and Transformers, (2) hypothesizing that heterogeneous timescale spectra aid hierarchical optimization and robustness, and (3) proposing concrete, low-cost architectural scaffolds and regularizers to produce multi-timescale behavior under tight parameter budgets.
Abstract: Residual connections and dynamical-isometry heuristics aim to make deep networks trainable by improving gradient/signal propagation. We propose a complementary structural principle: useful architectures should present a controlled repertoire of intrinsic timescales — block-level propagation modes that respond at different effective rates. Intuitively, fast modes quickly absorb and propagate high-frequency, low-level adjustments while slow modes act as stable integrators for higher-level features, enabling progressive, hierarchical credit assignment. We introduce practical estimators of intrinsic timescales (leading singular/eigenmodes of block Jacobians and their decay-like proxies via power-method J^T J sketches and randomized SVD) and simple, cheap architectural interventions: (i) timescale scaffolds — diagonal spectral gates per block initialized to span an exponential range of amplitudes, (ii) alternating fast/slow block patterns, and (iii) an early timescale-regularizer that encourages diversity of block spectral radii. We will test (a) whether initial timescale spectra predict trainability and robustness across ResNet-like CNNs and Transformer stacks, (b) whether timescale scaffolds accelerate convergence and improve robustness to weight perturbations and common corruptions, and (c) how these ideas interact with existing tricks (normalization, skip scaling). The interventions are parameter-efficient and implementable with a few extra scalars or tiny projections, making them practical for academic-scale experiments. Outcomes include diagnostics, prescriptive scaffolds, and a nuanced view of how structural spectral heterogeneity influences modern deep learning optimization.
Experiments: 1) Definitions and scalable estimators: treat each block-output as a state and estimate block propagation spectra by power-method sketches and randomized Jvps on J_block (input->output) to obtain top-k singular values and right-singular vectors. Define block timescale proxies (e.g., tau_b = 1 / (1 - sigma_max_b) or log-scale spectral amplitude) and a global Timescale Dispersion Index (TDI) across layers. Validate estimators vs exact SVD on small nets. 2) Predictive study: construct controlled families (plain deep stacks, ResNets with varied skip density/scale, Transformers) matched for depth and params. For each architecture measure initial TDI, per-block spectra, and correlate with trainability metrics: steps-to-target loss/accuracy (CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet), early-loss slope, gradient-norm stability, top Hessian eigenvalue, and robustness to random weight perturbations and CIFAR-C corruptions. Use Spearman/Pearson and partial correlations controlling for depth/params. 3) Timescale scaffolds (causal interventions): implement three low-cost interventions under tight budget (<1-5% params): (A) diagonal spectral gates g_b (per-block scalar or small diagonal) initialized to sample values spanning an exponential range (e.g., log-uniform across [1e-2,1]); (B) alternating fast/slow block design: alternate blocks with near-identity (slow) vs scaled (fast) initializations; (C) learned timescale gates with early regularizer R = -lambda * diversity(TDI) or smoothing penalty on spectral overlaps. Train variants on CIFAR/Tiny-ImageNet and compare convergence speed, final accuracy, gradient statistics, Hessian top eigenvalue, and robustness metrics to baselines (vanilla ResNet, LayerScale, added random skips). 4) Synthetic hierarchical-frequency tasks: design controlled regression/classification tasks where target function components have multiple frequency/time-constants (e.g., image textures + low-frequency shape) to test whether matching architectural timescales yields faster fitting of multi-scale targets. 5) Transformer study: apply block-level spectral gates to encoder stacks (attention+FFN blocks) on small LM tasks (WikiText-2) to test cross-family generality and interaction with pre-LN vs post-LN. 6) Ablations & comparisons: vary gate ranges, density (which blocks get gates), and compare to uniform dynamical-isometry initializations, LayerScale, and other predictors (effective resistance, Gramian metrics). Report compute overheads and parameter-efficiency trade-offs. Evaluation metrics: steps-to-target, final accuracy/perplexity, Spearman correlations, TDI changes during early epochs, robustness metrics, and additional per-parameter gain curves. All experiments feasible on single multi-GPU academic clusters.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Measurement validity: block Jacobian spectral proxies are linear approximations at initialization and may not capture nonlinear dynamics later; mitigate by focusing analyses on early training where initialization matters and sampling multiple inputs. 2) Redundancy to existing tricks: uniform scaling or LayerScale might already capture most benefits; the empirical novelty could be marginal. We'll compare directly and show parameter-efficiency differences. 3) Harmful rigidity: forcing fixed timescale diversity may obstruct later learning or reduce expressivity; use early-only regularizers and allow gates to adapt. 4) Hyperparameter sensitivity: ranges for initial gate spectrum and regularizer strength need tuning; include sensitivity sweeps and provide robust defaults. 5) Applicability limits: architectures lacking clear block decomposition (micro-NAS cells) or tasks without multi-scale structure may see little benefit. 6) Theoretical gaps: deriving rigorous convergence links from timescale spectra to optimization rates is challenging; emphasis is on empirical diagnostics, causal tests, and mechanistic intuition rather than formal proofs.

Name: mincut_capacity_trainability
Title: Min-Cut Capacity as a Structural Predictor and Design Tool for Trainable Deep Networks
Short Hypothesis: Treat a deep model's block-level computation graph as a directed flow network whose edge capacities measure local forward/backward information throughput (estimated from Jacobian/activation statistics). The input->output min-cut of this network predicts optimization ease, early learning speed, and perturbation robustness; explicitly increasing the min-cut via a few targeted, low-cost bypasses or capacity scalings yields disproportionately large improvements in trainability compared with indiscriminate skip additions. This setting isolates structural bottlenecks (cuts) rather than redundancy (parallel paths), making it the right lens to understand why some architectures stall despite many skips.
Related Work: Residual connections, DenseNets and highway networks (He et al., Huang et al.) are commonly explained via short paths or ensembles (Veit et al.). Mean-field/dynamical-isometry work (Poole, Schoenholz, Pennington et al.) studies signal/gradient propagation via spectra at initialization. The information-bottleneck literature and various layerwise capacity/expressivity studies consider representational compression, but they do not cast architecture as a flow network subject to min-cut bottlenecks. Prior structural metrics (shortest-path length, algebraic connectivity, effective resistance, Gramians, motif counts) either capture path redundancy, spectral balance, or local controllability; none explicitly measure directed bottlenecks that limit end-to-end throughput. Our proposal differs by (1) building a directed, capacity-weighted flow model of block-level computation using practical Jacobian/activation-based capacity proxies; (2) using max-flow/min-cut machinery to identify structural bottlenecks between input and output and to guide a tiny set of targeted interventions; and (3) empirically testing whether min-cut predicts and causally controls trainability and robustness across CNN and Transformer stacks. This is not a trivial variant of resistance/Gramian approaches because min-cut explicitly targets the narrowest bottleneck that limits end-to-end flow rather than global redundancy or local observability.
Abstract: Why do many deep architectures still struggle to learn even when equipped with numerous residuals or wide layers? We propose a simple structural lens: view the block-level architecture as a directed flow network and estimate per-edge information capacities from mini-batch Jacobian or activation statistics. The input->output min-cut of this network identifies the narrowest structural bottleneck that constrains both forward representation richness and backward credit assignment. We hypothesize (i) architectures with small min-cut (relative to depth/parameter budget) are slower to learn, more unstable, and less robust to perturbations; and (ii) targeted, low-cost capacity fixes at edges crossing that min-cut (gated bypasses, tiny projection skips, or short early scaling) raise trainability far more efficiently than adding unstructured skips. We develop practical capacity estimators (power-method spectral norm approximations of block Jacobians, block activation throughput proxies), scalable max-flow/min-cut computations on block graphs, and greedy min-cut–aware shortcut placement and early-capacity-warmup interventions. We validate across ResNet-like CNNs and Transformer encoder stacks on CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, and small language-modeling tasks, comparing min-cut predictions to other structural metrics (effective resistance, Gramians, motif counts). The outcome is an inexpensive structural diagnostic and prescriptive heuristics for where to add tiny bypasses or capacity scalings to make deep networks markedly more trainable and robust without large parameter overhead.
Experiments: 1) Define graph & capacity proxies: nodes = block outputs (residual block, transformer layer). Directed edges for feedforward links and explicit skips. Estimate edge capacity c_e via one of: (A) approximate spectral norm of the block Jacobian J (power-method with 1-3 iterations on a small batch) for edge mapping source->target; (B) Frobenius/Jacobian-vector average: E[||J v||^2] over Rademacher v; (C) activation-throughput proxy: mean L2 norm of target activations conditioned on source activation variance (cheap alternative). Validate proxies on small nets by comparing to full SVD on toy models to calibrate k and batch size. 2) Predictive correlation study: Assemble controlled families (plain deep stacks, ResNet variants with varied skip density/scale, Transformer encoder stacks) matched for depth/params. For each architecture compute min-cut (max-flow) between input and output and correlate (Spearman/Pearson) with trainability metrics: steps-to-target loss/accuracy (CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, WikiText-2 LM), early-loss slope, gradient-norm stability, frequency of exploding/vanishing events, top Hessian eigenvalue, and perturbation robustness (random weight noise, CIFAR-C corruptions). Include partial correlations controlling for depth/parameter count. 3) Min-cut–aware interventions (causal tests): For fixed small parameter budgets (e.g., +1-5% params), compare: (A) min-cut–guided bypass insertion: greedily add gated 1x1 conv/linear skips or tiny projection adapters across edges crossing the min-cut (choose edges that maximally increase min-cut estimate); (B) random bypass addition (same param budget); (C) naive long-skip additions. For each, train from scratch and measure steps-to-target, final accuracy/perplexity, gradient statistics, and robustness. 4) Early capacity warmup: instead of structural edits, test multiplicative early scaling of edges crossing min-cut (learnable gates initialized >1 or warmup schedule) and compare to min-cut bypasses and to standard warmup techniques. 5) Synthetic linearized tests: construct linear deep networks where analytic Jacobians and cut capacities can be computed; prove/illustrate how a narrow min-cut forces vanishing gradient energy and show how added bypasses across cut raise gradient throughput. 6) Comparison to other metrics: directly compare min-cut predictive power and intervention gains versus effective resistance, Gramian/Hankel summaries, gradient-frustration, motif counts, and simple metrics (depth, skip count). 7) Efficiency & robustness: profile runtime cost of capacity estimation and flow computation; test sensitivity to proxy choices & measurement batch sizes. All experiments feasible on academic multi-GPU hardware by restricting node granularity to blocks and using randomized, low-iteration estimators. Evaluation metrics: Spearman/Pearson correlations, steps-to-target, final accuracy/perplexity, gradient-norm & cosine statistics, top Hessian eigenvalue, robustness measures, and parameter-efficiency gain (accuracy per added param).
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Proxy mismatch: simple Jacobian/activation proxies may poorly estimate true 'information capacity'; to mitigate, we will compare multiple proxies, validate against exact SVDs on small models, and report sensitivity. 2) Confounding factors: min-cut may correlate strongly with width/parameter count; experiments will explicitly control for these and use partial correlations and matched-budget intervention tests. 3) Directedness & granularity: choosing nodes and directionality (symmetrization vs directed model) could affect cut computations; we will evaluate multiple graph encodings and report robustness. 4) Computational cost: repeated power-methods and flow solves add overhead; limit frequency (once per run or early-only), use 1-3 power iterations, and exploit small block graphs to keep costs low. 5) Non-uniqueness of interventions: raising min-cut via different mechanisms (scaling vs bypasses) may have different side effects; the study will compare options and prefer gated, small-parameter bypasses initialized benignly. 6) Limited scope: method is best suited to modular stacks with explicit block outputs (ResNets, Transformers) and may need adaptation for micro-NAS cells or heavily recurrent models. 7) Theoretical limits: rigorous bounds connecting min-cut values to convergence rates are challenging; emphasis is on practical, empirical diagnostic value and causal intervention studies rather than formal proofs.

Name: causal_influence_maps
Title: Causal Influence Maps: Measuring and Rewiring Layer-Level Causality to Improve Trainability
Short Hypothesis: Early, low-cost causal estimates of each block's influence on loss and gradient flow (a 'causal influence map' obtained via randomized, do-style interventions) predict later trainability and identify structural bottlenecks; using these maps to guide tiny, local interventions (gated bypasses, per-block learning-rate/optimizer scaling, or targeted skip addition) substantially improves convergence speed, stability, and robustness with minimal parameter overhead. This causal, block-level lens isolates structural effects that graph/spectral metrics miss and is feasible on modular stacks (ResNets, Transformers) where blocks and skips are explicit.
Related Work: Prior work probes architecture with ablations and linearization (mean-field / dynamical isometry), path- and ensemble-views of ResNets (Veit et al.), and graph/spectral predictors (effective resistance, Gramians, min-cut). 'Influence paths' (Lu et al., ACL 2020) analyze token/gate-level influence in LSTMs for linguistic phenomena, and influence functions study data-point effects on parameters. Our proposal differs fundamentally: we apply randomized, causal-style interventions to network blocks to estimate each block's causal effect on the training objective and on backward signal flow, then use these early causal maps to guide structural and optimizer interventions. This is not a trivial extension because (i) it treats blocks as manipulable causal units (do(block=0/noise/scale)), (ii) uses causal estimates to predict future optimization dynamics, and (iii) prescribes low-cost corrective actions that redistribute influence rather than only measuring it.
Abstract: Why do some layers or shortcuts make training fast while others are inert or harmful? We propose Causal Influence Maps: a simple, practical framework that (i) estimates the causal effect of each block (residual block, transformer layer) on the loss and on gradient propagation using randomized, do-style interventions (masking, scaled-noise injection, gated perturbations) measured on small minibatches at initialization and early training; (ii) compresses these measurements into a compact influence map that highlights bottleneck, redundant, and misaligned blocks; and (iii) uses that map to drive cheap, causal interventions—per-block learning-rate/optimizer preconditioning, gated bypasses placed across low-influence cuts, and early influence-aware pruning/gating—to improve convergence, stability, and robustness. We show that early causal-influence statistics predict steps-to-target loss, gradient instability events, and final generalization across CNN and Transformer families, and that influence-guided corrections outperform random or naive structural edits under equal parameter budgets. Our approach provides actionable diagnostics and prescriptive, low-cost architectural/optimizer remedies grounded in causal intervention rather than correlational heuristics.
Experiments: 1) Measurement protocol and estimators: define blocks as residual blocks / transformer layers. For a small held minibatch at init and early checkpoints, perform randomized interventions per block: (A) ablation (zeroing or stop-gradient), (B) scaled noise injection (multiply block output by s ∼ {0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5}), (C) gated probing (small learnable scalar initialized near 1 then perturbed). For each intervention compute (i) Δloss = E[loss|do(block)] - E[loss], (ii) Δoutput = L2 change in downstream activations, (iii) change in backward signal metrics (layerwise gradient norms, cosine alignment to full gradient), and (iv) short-run effects on a few SGD steps (loss trajectory for k steps after intervention). Aggregate to form per-block causal-influence scores (signed & magnitude). Use randomized assignment and repeated sampling to estimate uncertainty. Validate estimator reliability vs repeated trials and subsampled parameters.

2) Predictive study: construct controlled suites (plain stacks, ResNet variants, Transformer encoders) matched for depth/parameter count. Compute early causal influence maps and correlate (Spearman, partial correlations) with trainability outcomes: steps-to-target, early-loss slope, frequency of gradient explosions/vanishings, top Hessian eigenvalue, and final validation accuracy/perplexity on CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, and WikiText-2. Show influence maps add predictive power beyond depth, skip-count, effective-resistance, and Gramian measures.

3) Causal interventions (design experiments): for fixed small parameter/compute budgets, compare methods: (A) influence-guided bypass insertion: add gated 1×1 bypasses across edges that bridge low-influence bottlenecks (greedy maximize predicted reduction in Δloss), (B) per-block optimizer preconditioning: scale initial learning rates or Adam betas inversely to influence concentration (raise LR on low-influence blocks to encourage learning), (C) early gating/pruning of low-influence blocks (soft gate initialized small and optionally removed later). Baselines: random bypass insertion, uniform LR scaling, and standard tuning. Evaluate convergence speed, final accuracy/perplexity, gradient statistics, top Hessian eigenvalue, and robustness to weight perturbations and CIFAR-C corruptions.

4) Synthetic linearized theory tests: analyze linear deep networks where exact block causal effects are computable; prove that concentrated low-cut causal influence correlates with vanishing backward signal and slow convergence, and show how minimal bypasses raise influence spread and speed learning.

5) Ablations and generality: test sensitivity to intervention types (ablation vs noise), sampling frequency (init-only vs periodic), budget sizes, and applicability to pre-LN vs post-LN Transformers. Measure overhead and show practical defaults (e.g., 1–5% extra compute, infrequent sampling). Compare predictive/interventional gains to prior structural metrics (effective resistance, min-cut, Gramians, gradient-frustration).
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Measurement cost & noise: randomized interventions require extra forward/backward work and may be noisy. Mitigation: subsample minibatches, use few interventions per epoch, and aggregate across repeats to control variance.

2) Nonstationarity: early causal estimates might change as parameters evolve; mitigate by focusing on initialization and very early epochs (where many architecture effects manifest) and optionally re-evaluating infrequently.

3) Confounding dynamics: interventions change network state potentially causing downstream adaptation; use randomized assignment and small perturbations, and validate causality by measuring short-run dynamics and repeated trials.

4) Destructive interventions: naive ablations can destabilize training. Use gated/soft interventions initialized benignly and unfreeze gradually; prefer small scaling or noise probes over hard removals.

5) Scope: requires modular block structure to define manipulable units; may not directly apply to micro-NAS cells or non-modular designs without a clear mapping. We will report granularity sensitivity and provide block-level defaults.

6) Limited theoretical guarantees: deriving tight optimization bounds from causal influence maps is challenging; the contribution is empirical, diagnostic, and prescriptive with mechanistic linearized justification.

Name: subnetwork_diversity
Title: Multiplicity vs Diversity: Subnetwork Diversity as a Predictor and Design Objective for Trainable Deep Networks
Short Hypothesis: Residual architectures realize implicit ensembles of subnetworks (multiplicity). But multiplicity alone is not sufficient: the functional diversity among these subnetworks — measured as differences in outputs, representations, and gradient directions across sampled subnetworks — predicts optimization ease, stability, and robustness better than raw path counts. Lightweight, parameter-efficient interventions that increase subnetwork diversity early in training (diversity-regularized gating, path-specialized adapters, or curated path-sampling curricula) accelerate convergence and improve robustness more per added parameter than naïve extra skips or deeper stacks. The block-level residual/skip setting (ResNet-like stacks and Transformer residual blocks) is the right testbed because subnetworks are explicit and cheaply sampled via standard stochastic-depth/path-drop mechanisms.
Related Work: Veit et al. (2016) interpreted ResNets as exponential ensembles of relatively shallow subnetworks; stochastic depth and DropPath sample such subnetworks during training. Ensemble diversity literature (negative correlation learning, diversity-promoting regularizers) shows that ensemble members' disagreement can improve generalization.
