Multi-branch designs (ResNeXt, Inception) and sparsely-gated MoEs encourage specialization across routes. Yet, prior work has not (1) systematically quantified the functional and gradient diversity of implicit residual subnetworks as a predictor of trainability and robustness, (2) compared diversity vs multiplicity (path-count) across architecture families, nor (3) proposed and evaluated cheap architectural/regularization mechanisms that causally increase subnetwork diversity under tight parameter budgets. This proposal fills that gap: it measures subnetwork diversity via sampled path distributions, relates it to optimization dynamics, and prescribes low-cost diversity interventions tailored to residual/shortcut architectures. This is not a trivial extension of Veit or stochastic depth because we move from descriptive ensemble views to quantitative diversity diagnostics and causal, resource-efficient design rules.
Abstract: Residual connections unlock very deep architectures by implicitly creating ensembles of many subnetworks. But why do some residual designs learn faster and generalize better even when the nominal multiplicity (number of paths) is similar? We propose that the key structural quantity is subnetwork diversity: how functionally different are the subnetworks that compose the implicit ensemble? We develop scalable measures of diversity for residual/shortcut stacks — output variance across sampled subnetworks, representational dissimilarity (CCA/CKA) among subnetwork outputs, gradient-direction diversity, and ensemble entropy on predictions — and show these measures, computed at initialization and during early training, strongly predict optimization speed, stability, and robustness across ResNet variants and Transformer stacks. Building on these diagnostics, we introduce three inexpensive, practical interventions that raise diversity per added parameter: (i) diversity-regularized gating — learnable per-skip gates trained with a lightweight repulsive loss on sampled subnetworks' outputs; (ii) path-specialized adapters — tiny path-specific projection adapters that encourage route specialization; and (iii) path-sampling curricula — early training schedules that enforce varied subnetwork draws to avoid dominant shallow routes. Across CIFAR/Tiny-ImageNet and small language-modeling tasks, we show that increasing subnetwork diversity accelerates learning, reduces gradient collapse events, and improves robustness to weight noise and distribution shift more effectively than simply increasing skip density or depth. The outcomes are (a) a compact suite of diversity diagnostics for architects and (b) low-cost, causal design rules for making multiplicity useful rather than redundant.
Experiments: 1) Definitions & scalable estimators: Define a subnetwork as the set of residual branches activated by a binary mask (stochastic depth or DropPath sampling). Sample S subnetworks (S=32-128) per input minibatch cheaply by using standard stochastic-depth masks. Compute diversity measures: (A) predictive diversity: average pairwise KL or prediction entropy among subnetworks' softmax outputs; (B) representational diversity: average pairwise CKA/CCA between final-layer representations produced by subnetworks; (C) gradient diversity: average pairwise cosine between gradients of loss w.r.t shared parameters when backpropagated through different subnetworks (use vector-Jacobian products on a small parameter subset to be cheap); (D) functional variance: variance over subnetworks of logits on a held validation mini-batch. Validate estimator stability vs S and mini-batch size and provide low-cost defaults (S~32, small held-batch of 128 examples).

2) Correlation study across architectures: Build families of architectures with controlled depth/param budgets: plain deep stacks, ResNet variants with varied skip density/strength (standard, scaled residuals, stochastic depth rates), and ResNeXt-like grouped blocks. For Transformers, test encoder stacks with/without residual scaling and with different dropout patterns. For each architecture, measure initial and early-training diversity metrics and correlate (Spearman) with trainability outcomes: steps-to-target loss/accuracy on CIFAR-10/100 and Tiny-ImageNet, early-loss descent slope, frequency of gradient instability events, and final validation accuracy/perplexity. Compare predictive power of diversity metrics to multiplicity/path-count, effective resistance, and Gramian/Hankel measures using partial correlations controlling for depth/params.

3) Diversity interventions (causal tests): Implement three low-overhead interventions and compare under matched parameter budgets: (A) Diversity-regularized gating: add scalar gates to residual branches; during early training uniformly sample K subnetworks per batch and add a small repulsive loss L_div = lambda * sum_{i<j} ReLU( c0 - cosine(repr_i, repr_j) ) on sampled subnetwork representations (or negative pairwise CKA) to push outputs apart. Gates are learnable but regularizer is only applied early (first E epochs) to seed specialization. (B) Path-specialized adapters: add tiny (bottleneck) adapters per block that are only used when a branch is active (adapters size tuned so total added params <=2-3%). These adapters give paths a small dedicated parameter budget to specialize. (C) Path-sampling curricula: enforce diverse path draws early by biasing stochastic-depth sampling to favor rarer longer paths (e.g., temperature-adjusted Bernoulli sampling) then anneal to usual rates. Train each variant and baselines (vanilla ResNet, stochastic depth, added random skips, scaled residuals) and evaluate steps-to-target, final accuracy/perplexity, gradient diversity evolution, Hessian top eigenvalue, and robustness under random weight perturbations and CIFAR-C/common corruptions.

4) Ablations & efficiency: Vary S, lambda, adapter sizes, and curriculum schedules. Test whether diversity gains transfer to held-out datasets and to transfer learning few-shot tasks. Measure extra compute and wall-time, showing interventions add modest overhead (<10-20% training time depending on estimator frequency) and small parameter cost. Evaluate whether enforced diversity harms final performance (over-specialization) by sweeping regularizer strength and unfreezing gates after early phase.

5) Synthetic controlled tasks & linear theory: Construct toy linear residual ensembles where analytic subnetwork functions are tractable (e.g., concatenated linear blocks with identity skips) to show how path multiplicity with low diversity leads to redundant gradient directions and slow convergence, and how small adapters or repulsive losses increase subnetwork functional rank and accelerate closed-form gradient descent convergence. Use these models to derive intuition linking representational diversity to condition number of effective Jacobians.

6) Transformer study: Apply the most promising interventions (gating + small adapters) to transformer encoder stacks on WikiText-2 / PTB and small GLUE-style tasks to test cross-family generality. Measure per-block subnetwork diversity where a subnetwork corresponds to different dropout/skip realizations for attention/FFN residuals.

Evaluation metrics: Spearman/Pearson correlations of diversity vs trainability, steps-to-target, final accuracy, early-loss slope, gradient-instability frequency, robustness measures (weight-noise tolerance, CIFAR-C error), parameter-efficiency (gain per added parameter), and compute overhead.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Measurement cost and noise: sampling many subnetworks and computing pairwise metrics can be costly; mitigate via subsampling (S~32), small held-batches and infrequent evaluation (once per N steps) and cheap approximations (random projection for CKA).

2) Correlation vs causation: diversity might correlate with other beneficial properties; interventions (adapters, gating + repulsive loss, curricula) are explicitly designed to test causality, but effects may be modest.

3) Over-specialization risk: forcing subnetworks apart could reduce shared learning or lead to underfitting; mitigations include applying diversity pressure only early and keeping strength small, and allowing gates/adapters to adapt later.
4) Applicability: notion of 'subnetwork' is natural for residual/skipped stacks but less clear for tightly coupled or cell-based NAS architectures; methods may need adaptation.

5) Evaluation subtleties: diversity measures (e.g., representational CKA/CCA) have hyperparameters (projection dims, batch sizes) that affect stability; the paper will report sensitivity analyses and recommended defaults.

6) Marginal gains: in some settings, multiplicity may already be sufficient and additional diversity provides little improvement; the study will characterize regimes (depth, dataset scale, initialization) where diversity matters most.

Name: architectural_continuation
Title: Architectural Continuation: Homotopy Paths from Trainable Scaffolds to Expressive Architectures
Short Hypothesis: Instead of fixing a network's computational graph from the start, optimize along a continuous homotopy that morphs a simple, easy-to-train scaffold into the final expressive architecture. Properly designed morphing schedules (continuous interpolation of operators, edges, or nonlinearity strength) produce smoother early loss landscapes, reduce optimization stalls, and yield equal-or-better final performance and robustness with minimal extra cost compared to static architectures or discrete Net2Net-style transforms. This directly tests whether the trainability gap between architectures can be closed by following a continuous architectural path rather than only changing initializations or adding heuristics.
Related Work: Relevant predecessors include Net2Net (function-preserving widening/adding layers), progressive growing in GANs (Karras et al.) and progressive resizing/curriculum learning (data-side continuation), LayerScale / residual scaling and warmup tricks that modify dynamics, and homotopy/continuation methods from optimization (graduated non-convexity, smoothing). None of these systematically treat the architecture itself as a continuous homotopy parameterized and scheduled during optimization. Net2Net preserves function via discrete reparameterizations and requires careful transfer; progressive GAN growth focuses on generation and resolution, not general architectural operator interpolation; continuation in loss or data distribution is common, but architectural homotopy—continuous interpolation of operations, skip weights, or nonlinearity amplitude from an 'easy' scaffold to the target architecture—has not been explored as a general, lightweight design for trainability. This proposal differs by: (1) defining continuous morphings of computation (edge gating, operator interpolation, linearization-to-nonlinearity annealing) at block granularity; (2) proposing principled schedules (time-based, metric-based) and cheap adaptive rules; and (3) providing a causal empirical study across CNNs and Transformers comparing to static baselines, Net2Net transfers, and other stabilizing techniques.
Abstract: Many architectural tricks (residuals, normalization, skip patterns) improve trainability, but we rarely let the architecture itself change during the main optimization except via expensive reparameterizations. We propose architectural continuation: treat architecture as a time-varying object and follow a continuous homotopy from a simple, highly-trainable scaffold to the final expressive model. Constructions include (A) operator interpolation: block outputs are convex combinations y = (1-s(t)) y_scaffold + s(t) y_target where y_scaffold is identity/linearized/simple-block output and y_target is the full nonlinear block; (B) edge-graph homotopy: start with a dense identity-dominated scaffold and gradually shift weights toward the target residual/skip graph via gated edges; (C) nonlinearity annealing: scale nonlinear activations (or attention sharpness) from near-linear to full nonlinearity. We hypothesize these homotopies smooth the loss landscape early, reduce harmful stationary regions, and allow simple SGD dynamics to reach basins that map to strong final solutions. The project develops simple, scalable morphing primitives and schedules (linear, cosine, and gradient-adaptive), theoretical intuition in linearized toy models showing how homotopy removes spurious minima, and an experimental campaign across ResNet-like CNNs and Transformer encoders on CIFAR / Tiny-ImageNet and small LM tasks. We compare to static training, Net2Net-style discrete transforms, and existing stabilizing tricks. The outcome is a lightweight, widely-applicable methodology and prescriptive schedules to make deep architectures more trainable without large parameter or compute overhead.
Experiments:
1) Primitive definitions & implementations (block granularity):
   - Operator interpolation: implement each block as y = (1-s(t)) * y_scaffold + s(t) * y_target. y_scaffold can be identity, a linear projection, or a simplified block (e.g., linearized conv + skip). y_target is the usual block (conv/BN/ReLU/residual or attention+FFN). s(t) → 0 at init, s(T)=1 at end.
   - Edge homotopy: represent the block graph with gated edges g_e(t) that interpolate from scaffold topology (dense identity shortcuts) to final topology (desired residual graph). Implement gates as scheduled scalars or learnable parameters with scheduled priors.
   - Nonlinearity annealing: multiply block nonlinearities by factor alpha(t) ∈ [0,1], starting near 0 (almost linear) and annealing to 1.
   Implementation notes: keep extra parameters minimal (per-block scalars or tiny projection adapters). Ensure BN/running stats handled by maintaining scaffold statistics or using small hold-in periods.

2) Schedule families & adaptive rules:
   - Time-based: linear, cosine, exponential schedules parameterized by total morph epochs T_m.
   - Metric-based: advance s(t) when training loss or gradient-norm reaches thresholds; or use curvature cue (top Hessian eigenvalue estimate via power iteration) to delay morphing until local curvature reduces.
   - Hybrid: fast initial morph for shallow blocks, slower for deep blocks (staged morphing).

3) Baselines & comparisons:
   - Static target architecture trained normally.
   - Net2Net-style discrete morphs (widen then continue), progressive resizing, LayerScale and standard warmup.
   - Ablations: only operator interpolation, only edge homotopy, only nonlinearity annealing, and combinations.

4) Tasks & architectures:
   - CNNs: ResNet-50/101-like stacks and deeper plain stacks on CIFAR-10/100 and Tiny-ImageNet; small ImageNet subset for scale check.
   - Transformers: encoder stacks for language modeling (WikiText-2) and small sequence classification tasks.

5) Measurements & evaluation metrics:
   - Optimization: steps-to-target training loss, early-loss descent slope (first 10k steps), frequency of stalled epochs.
   - Final generalization: validation accuracy / perplexity.
   - Stability & geometry: layerwise gradient norms & variance, top Hessian eigenvalue, loss-landscape sharpness (filter-normalized perturbations), and presence of flat directions.
   - Robustness: sensitivity to random weight perturbations, CIFAR-C common corruptions, and adversarial-based sanity checks.
   - Cost: added parameters, wall-clock overhead, and sensitivity to T_m.

6) Toy-theory & linearized experiments:
   - Linear deep networks where homotopy corresponds to convex interpolation between matrices; analyze stationary points and gradient flow to show homotopy can remove spurious minima or widen basins.
   - Small experiments demonstrating morphing reduces gradient decay analytically and empirically.

7) Practical recommendations:
   - Provide default schedules and primitive choices (e.g., identity scaffold + cosine anneal of s(t) over first 20% epochs) that work across families.

All experiments are designed to be feasible on academic multi-GPU clusters by keeping morph primitives lightweight (per-block scalars or tiny adapters) and by limiting expensive diagnostics (Hessian power iterations) to infrequent probes.
Risk Factors and Limitations:
1) Schedule sensitivity: poorly chosen morphing schedules may harm final convergence or cause forgetting; mitigate by testing simple robust schedules (cosine, staged morphing) and adaptive rules.
2) Extra complexity & overhead: morphing adds control logic and occasional extra forward/backward work; keep overhead minimal (scalar gates) and measure wall-clock trade-offs.
3) Interaction with normalization: BN running stats may mismatch between scaffold and target behaviors; mitigate with small warmup windows, use of GroupNorm/LayerNorm alternatives, or maintaining separate statistics during scaffold phase.
4) Nonlinearity irreversibility: some morphological steps (e.g., introducing attention patterns) may create dead directions if introduced too late; design scaffolds that already expose necessary subspaces (e.g., small projection adapters) or use staged morphing.
5) Limited universality: improvements may be strongest in very deep or poorly conditioned architectures and less pronounced on already-easy models; report regimes of efficacy and negative results.
6) Theoretical limits: while linearized analyses provide intuition, producing rigorous global convergence guarantees for nonconvex architectures under architectural homotopy is challenging; emphasis is empirical, practical, and mechanistic rather than purely theoretical.

Name: symmetry_breaking_scaffolds
Title: Symmetry-Breaking Scaffolds: Minimal Deterministic Asymmetries for Trainable Deep Networks
Short Hypothesis: Deep architectures with repeated, near-identical blocks suffer optimization slowdowns because permutation and feature-symmetries create degenerate, flat directions. Introducing tiny, deterministic, data-aware asymmetries at the block level (minimal low-rank adapters, spectral micro-rotations, or learned per-block gates) — scheduled and calibrated early in training — breaks harmful symmetry in a controlled way, markedly improving convergence speed, stability, and robustness at negligible parameter cost. This hypothesis isolates structural symmetry effects from other explanations (path length, dynamical isometry) by operating at block granularity and comparing to random noise baselines and existing residual/scaling tricks.
Related Work: Prior work has observed that symmetry-related singularities slow training and that residuals often mitigate such pathologies (Orhan & Pitkow, ICLR 2017: "Skip Connections Eliminate Singularities"). Blumenfeld et al. (ICML 2020) showed that identical-feature initializations can still train if nondeterministic hardware breaks symmetry, indicating the importance of symmetry-breaking but leaving open principled, reproducible interventions. Other literature addresses signal/gradient propagation (mean-field/dynamical isometry), ensemble and subnetwork effects (Veit et al.), and architectural regularizers, but none provide a compact, deterministic, data-aware toolkit for minimal symmetry breaking together with diagnostics and schedules. Our proposal differs by (1) formalizing block-level permutation/feature symmetry and providing cheap symmetry diagnostics, (2) introducing several small, deterministic, parameter-efficient symmetry-breaking primitives (data-aligned low-rank adapters, micro-rotations, learned gates with annealed bias) that are explicitly designed to be minimal and reversible, and (3) testing causal efficacy via controlled symmetric initializations and matched-budget interventions across CNN and Transformer stacks. This is not a trivial restatement of prior observations: we move from anecdotal or hardware-noise reliance to prescriptive, reproducible architectural primitives and metrics for when and how to break symmetry.
Abstract: Many deep architectures contain repeated modules (residual blocks, transformer layers) that introduce approximate permutation and feature symmetries. These symmetries create degenerate parameter manifolds and flat directions that can slow early training or induce unstable dynamics. We propose a principled, practical program: (i) measure block-level symmetry via compact diagnostics (pairwise representational similarity, a "symmetry index," and permutation-compatibility scores computed by cheap CKA/CCA sketches); (ii) design minimal deterministic symmetry-breaking primitives — data-aligned low-rank adapters, tiny block-specific spectral micro-rotations, and learnable scalar gates with small initial offsets — that add negligible parameters but orient blocks differently in data-relevant subspaces; (iii) schedule and tune these primitives early in training (anneal strength, optionally unfreeze) to avoid harming final expressivity; and (iv) validate causally by training deliberately symmetric networks (cloned-block initializations) and showing that symmetry-aware primitives improve convergence, stability, and robustness compared to random perturbations, skip-scaling, and other baselines. We develop scalable estimators to detect harmful symmetry, provide heuristics for minimal adapter budgets, and analyze mechanisms via linearized models that show how small asymmetric low-rank components lift degeneracies and increase effective Jacobian rank. Experiments across ResNet-like CNNs and Transformer encoders on CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, and small language-modeling tasks will demonstrate that deterministic, data-aware symmetry breaking is a low-cost, general architectural tool to make deep networks more trainable and robust.
Experiments: 1) Symmetry diagnostics and operational definitions: define block nodes (residual block outputs, transformer layer outputs). Compute pairwise representational similarity using cheap, randomized CKA/CCA sketches on a small held mini-batch to form an SxS similarity matrix (S = number of blocks). From this derive a Symmetry Index (SI) = fraction of block pairs with similarity > tau and a Permutation Compatibility Score (PCS) via clustering the similarity matrix. Validate stability of these diagnostics vs batch size and sketch dims. 2) Controlled symmetric baselines: construct purposely symmetric architectures by cloning an initial block (copy weights) across many depths and (A) leaving exact clones frozen at init or (B) allowing normal SGD. Use these to create a canonical failure mode where hardware-noise-based symmetry breaking is absent. Measure baseline training dynamics, gradient spectra, Hessian top eigenvalues, and SI evolution. 3) Symmetry-breaking primitives (implementation details): (A) Data-aligned rank-1 adapters: add per-block adapter y <- y + eps * u_b (v^T y) where u_b is a small learned vector initialized to top data PC for that block (computed on a small batch) and v is shared random probe vector. Total params per block = O(dim). eps small (e.g., 1e-3-1e-2). (B) Micro-rotation/skew transforms: apply y <- y + eps * (a_b (w^T y)) with a_b learned per-block direction and w a fixed probe; or implement tiny skew-symmetric param S_b in a low-dim subspace using two vectors to approximate small rotations. (C) Symmetry-aware scalar gates: per-skip or per-block scalar g_b initialized to 1 + delta_b where small deterministic offsets delta_b are chosen via a simple balanced schedule or data-PC alignment. (D) Symmetry-aware optimizer scaling: per-block LR multipliers inversely proportional to local similarity (raise LR for overly similar blocks). All primitives are budgeted so added params < 1-5% of model. 4) Intervention protocols & baselines: for cloned symmetric models, compare: (i) random tiny perturbations to cloned weights (Gaussian noise), (ii) hardware-like non-deterministic runs (control), (iii) each symmetry-breaking primitive above, (iv) combinations and annealed schedules (apply only in first E epochs and optionally decay). Baselines include standard ResNet/Transformer training, LayerScale/skip-scaling, stochastic depth, and low-rank adapters not aligned to data. 5) Measurements & metrics: primary optimization metrics: steps-to-target training loss, early-loss slope, frequency of stalled epochs. Secondary metrics: final validation accuracy/perplexity, gradient covariance rank and top eigenvalue, Hessian top eigenvalue, Symmetry Index evolution, robustness measures (random weight perturbation tolerance, CIFAR-C corruptions), and parameter-efficiency (gain per added parameter). 6) Mechanistic analyses: (A) Linearized toy models: analyze deep linear stacks with identical block matrices and show analytically how adding a rank-1 asymmetric perturbation lifts degeneracy in the Hessian and speeds convergence; (B) empirical singular-value tracking: measure how symmetry-breaking primitives increase effective Jacobian rank and spread singular values across blocks during early training. 7) Cross-family tests and ablations: repeat main protocols for (i) ResNet-style CNNs on CIFAR-10/100 and Tiny-ImageNet, (ii) Transformer encoders on WikiText-2 / PTB small LM and a small classification task, and (iii) sensitivity sweeps over eps, adapter rank, SI threshold tau, and annealing window E. 8) Practical guidelines: from experiments derive simple rules: minimal adapter rank for given block dims, recommended eps, and whether to anneal or keep adapters trainable. All measurement-heavy diagnostics are run infrequently (e.g., once per epoch) and use small held minibatches to keep overhead low; experiments are sized to fit single multi-GPU academic clusters.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Scope of effect: harmful symmetry is most obvious for deliberately cloned or highly-repetitive blocks; on typical random initializations natural asymmetry may already be enough, leaving modest practical gains. We mitigate by focusing both on artificially symmetric settings (to prove causality) and on real-world architectures to quantify effect size. 2) Overfitting to data PCs: data-aligned adapters risk biasing early representations to idiosyncratic dataset directions. Mitigate by using very small adapter strength (eps), low adapter rank, computing PCs on held/unlabeled batches, and annealing off early. 3) Hyperparameter tuning: adapter rank, eps, gate offsets, and anneal schedules add knobs. We will report sensitivity sweeps and provide robust defaults (e.g., rank-1, eps in [1e-3,1e-2], anneal over first 5-20 epochs). 4) Potential loss of beneficial symmetry: some symmetry (e.g., repeated processing that is useful) could be helpful; breaking it indiscriminately might reduce final performance. To avoid harm we emphasize minimal, reversible primitives and early-only application. 5) Measurement cost: computing CKA/CCA sketches, PCs, and singular-value diagnostics adds overhead; we will use randomized sketching, small held batches, and infrequent evaluation (once per N epochs) to stay within academic compute budgets. 6) Theoretical limitations: formalizing permutation symmetry groups for arbitrary architectures and proving tight convergence bounds under symmetry-breaking is challenging; the contribution is primarily empirical, diagnostic, and mechanistic with supporting linearized analyses rather than full formal guarantees.

Name: orthogonal_gradient_subspaces
Title: Orthogonal Gradient Subspaces: Reducing Inter-Block Interference to Improve Trainability
Short Hypothesis: Interference between gradient signals arriving at different blocks (or along different residual paths) collapses the effective optimization basis and worsens conditioning. Enforcing or encouraging orthogonality among leading block-level gradient subspaces at initialization and early training (via cheap, mini-batch based subspace estimates and lightweight orthogonalization losses or projection layers) expands the effective gradient basis, reduces ill-conditioning, and thereby accelerates convergence, stabilizes training, and improves perturbation robustness across ResNet- and Transformer-style modular stacks. This is the cleanest setting to test the hypothesis because block-output gradients are compact, cheaply observable, and directly tied to backward credit assignment, allowing low-cost interventions that isolate structural effects from mere parameter-count changes.
Related Work: Multi-task gradient surgery (PCGrad), GradNorm and other multi-task methods explicitly manipulate conflicting gradients across tasks; these manipulate full parameter gradients and target task conflicts. DeCov and similar methods decorrelate activations, and orthogonal-weight regularizers encourage orthonormal weights for better signal propagation. Signal/gradient propagation analyses (dynamical isometry, Jacobian spectra, Gramian/Hankel) study singular-value distributions but do not directly target inter-block gradient subspace overlap. Gradient-frustration (our earlier proposal) measures per-branch signed conflicts; by contrast, this work (1) focuses on the subspace geometry of block-output gradients (dL/ds) rather than pairwise angle statistics, (2) prescribes constructive orthogonalization/regularization that increases the span of gradient directions rather than only reducing pairwise conflict, and (3) provides cheap, randomized estimators tailored to block outputs so overhead is small. To our knowledge, no prior work has proposed orthogonalizing block-level gradient subspaces as a structural design/regularization principle for trainability.
Abstract: Why are some deep modular stacks (ResNets, Transformers) so much easier to optimize? We propose a new, structural explanation and practical remedy: at initialization and in early training, gradient signals that reach different blocks or travel along different residual routes often lie in overlapping low-dimensional subspaces. This overlap reduces the effective optimization basis, increases condition numbers, and causes slow or unstable learning. We introduce Orthogonal Gradient Subspaces (OGS): a lightweight framework to (i) measure block-level gradient subspaces cheaply using mini-batch dL/ds vectors and randomized subspace sketches, (ii) quantify subspace overlap and show it predicts optimization difficulty beyond depth and parameter count, and (iii) causally improve trainability by (A) a short-duration orthogonality regularizer that penalizes overlap among top-k block-gradient bases estimated on a small sample, and (B) tiny projection/adapter modules per block that are orthogonalized (Gram-Schmidt style) to previously placed bases at init or early epochs. Both interventions are designed to add minimal parameters and compute and to be applied only early to avoid harming later specialization. We validate across CNN and Transformer families on CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet and small language-modeling tasks, showing faster early loss descent, fewer instability events, improved robustness to parameter perturbations, and superior parameter-efficiency compared to naive skip additions, scaled residuals, and gradient-frustration baselines. OGS yields actionable diagnostics and prescriptive, low-cost design rules for building more trainable deep networks.
Experiments: 1) Measurement & estimators: instrument architectures to capture block-output gradients g_b = dL/ds_b on small held mini-batches at init and early epochs. Use randomized subspace iteration / thin SVD (k=4-16) on accumulated g_b vectors to produce orthonormal bases P_b. Compute pairwise subspace overlaps via principal angles or overlap metric O_ij = ||P_i^T P_j||_F^2 and a global Overlap Score (sum_{i<j} O_ij). Validate estimator stability vs batch size and k, and show overhead profiling. 2) Predictive study: build controlled architecture suites: plain deep stacks, ResNet variants (varying skip density/scale), and Transformer encoder stacks, matched for depth and parameter count. For each compute early Overlap Score and correlate (Spearman/partial correlations controlling for depth/params) with trainability outcomes: steps-to-target, early-loss slope, frequency of gradient explosions/vanishings, final validation accuracy/perplexity on CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, and WikiText-2. Compare predictive power to simpler metrics (skip-count, effective resistance, Gramian trace, gradient-frustration). 3) Causal interventions — orthogonality regularizer: implement L_ortho = lambda * sum_{i<j} ||Proj_{P_i}(P_j)||_F^2 where P_b are bases estimated from fresh mini-batch g_b samples; compute on small k and apply only during early epochs (e.g., first 5-10 epochs) with stop-gradient on basis computation to avoid collapse. Evaluate variants: symmetric pairwise penalty vs sequential Gram-Schmidt penalty. Measure convergence speed, final accuracy, gradient-norm statistics, top Hessian eigenvalue, and robustness (random weight perturbations, CIFAR-C), and ablate lambda and k. 4) Causal interventions — orthogonal adapters/projections: add tiny per-block adapters (bottleneck 1x1 convs or low-rank linear layers) whose columns are orthogonalized sequentially at initialization to form an explicit orthonormal basis across blocks (Gram-Schmidt on small random vectors or data-informed directions). Keep adapter param budget small (<=2% params). Train with adapters frozen for early epochs or with a small orthogonality penalty to maintain diversity. Compare to same-size random adapters and to adding naive skips. 5) Mechanistic linear tests: construct linear deep networks where block-output dimensions and gradient flows are analytically tractable; show how subspace overlap reduces effective condition numbers and how orthogonalization increases effective rank and speeds gradient descent. 6) Cross-family tests: replicate best interventions on Transformer encoder stacks (attention+FFN residuals) for small LM tasks (WikiText-2) using per-layer token-embedding gradient subspaces. 7) Ablations and safety checks: test whether early-only application vs continual application is better; measure possible harms (suppression of beneficial coordination), test on varied datasets and widths, and quantify compute/param overhead. Evaluation metrics: steps-to-target, early-loss slope, Spearman correlations, overlap reduction, Hessian top eigenvalue, robustness metrics, and wall-clock overhead.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Measurement & compute overhead: computing subspace bases requires extra storage and SVD/Jvps; mitigate via small k (4-8), tiny held batches, randomized subspace iteration, and infrequent evaluations (once per N steps). 2) Harmful over-orthogonalization: forcing orthogonality may prevent beneficial coordinated gradients (shared updates), harming final performance. Mitigation: apply only during the early phase, use small lambda, and unfreeze/remove penalty mid-training. 3) Noise and estimator bias: small-batch g_b estimates may be noisy; use averaging over a few batches and report uncertainty; test sensitivity to batch size. 4) Applicability scope: method assumes modular blocks with observable block outputs; micro-NAS cells or highly entangled parameterizations may need adapted node definitions. 5) Causality risk: overlap might correlate with other structural pathologies rather than cause them; we include causal interventions (regularizer and adapters) to test and quantify direct effects. 6) Theoretical limits: deriving rigorous global convergence bounds from subspace-orthogonality is challenging; we focus on empirical demonstration, mechanistic linearized analysis, and practical heuristics.

Name: hessian_aligned_bypasses
Title: Hessian-Aligned Bypasses: Structural Preconditioning via Low-Rank Curvature-Directed Skips
Short Hypothesis: A small number of low-rank bypass modules, initialized to act in the dominant early Hessian eigen-directions and applied as gated residuals, function as structural preconditioners that reduce harmful sharp curvature, improve gradient conditioning, and accelerate/stabilize training across CNN and Transformer families. Estimating a few top Hessian eigenvectors early in training and embedding inexpensive bypasses aligned to those directions yields larger per-parameter trainability and robustness gains than generic skip-addition or optimizer-only second-order methods, because the architecture itself shapes propagation and credit assignment rather than merely changing update rules.
Related Work: Empirical analyses of Hessian spectra in deep nets (Sagun et al., Ghorbani et al.) show a small number of dominant eigenmodes linked to sharpness and generalization. Second-order optimizers and preconditioners (KFAC, Shampoo) and spectral regularizers attempt to address curvature via update rules. Architectural approaches (residuals, LayerScale, skip-scaling) improve conditioning but are not explicitly curvature-aware. Low-rank adapters and LoRA introduce small parameter-efficient modules for adaptation but are data- or task-oriented rather than curvature-directed. To our knowledge, no prior work embeds curvature compensation into the computational graph as tiny, low-rank, Hessian-aligned bypass modules. Our proposal is novel because it (1) treats architecture as a vehicle for preconditioning by inserting bypasses aligned with dominant Hessian eigenvectors estimated at initialization/early training; (2) shows how to do this cheaply and robustly with noisy eigenvector estimates (few Lanczos steps, randomized probes); and (3) evaluates causal effects across architectures, contrasting with optimizer-based preconditioning and naive skip additions.
Abstract: Sharp directions in the loss Hessian at initialization or in early training correlate with slow or unstable optimization and brittle generalization. We propose a new, structural remedy: embed tiny, low-rank bypass modules into a deep network whose projection directions are initialized to approximate the dominant early Hessian eigenvectors, and whose gains are gated and annealed during training. These 'Hessian-aligned bypasses' act as architectural preconditioners: by routing a small fraction of signal through directions that directly target high-curvature subspaces, they reduce effective sharpness, improve gradient conditioning, and make gradient descent dynamics more stable without large parameter or optimizer changes. We develop practical estimators for the top-k Hessian eigenvectors using a small number of Hessian-vector (Lanczos/power) probes on tiny mini-batches, design low-cost bypass modules (rank-r projections implemented as bottleneck 1x1 convs or low-rank linear maps with shared components) and gating schedules that avoid disrupting expressivity, and derive simple initialization rules that damp top curvature rather than canceling it. Through experiments on ResNet-like CNNs and Transformer encoder stacks (CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, WikiText-2), we (i) quantify how early Hessian eigendirections concentrate across architectures, (ii) show that Hessian-aligned bypasses shrink top eigenvalues and speed early loss descent and final convergence more per added parameter than naive skip addition or early-second-order optimizer use, and (iii) analyze robustness improvements under weight perturbations and common corruptions. The result is a compact, practical structural tool for curvature-aware architecture design.
Experiments: 1) Practical estimators & module design: implement a lightweight Hessian-top-k estimator using 4-8 Lanczos / power iterations on Hessian-vector products approximated over tiny minibatches (e.g., 128 examples) and validate estimator stability vs iterations and batch size on small models. Design bypass modules: rank-r (r=1..8) projections P and Q so bypass(x) = Q (diag(g) * (P^T x)), where P projects onto estimated top eigen-directions and Q maps back to block-output space; implement as tiny bottleneck 1x1 convs (CNN) or low-rank linear layers (Transformer). Bypasses are gated by a per-module scalar initialized small and optionally scheduled/learned. 2) Baseline correlation study: measure initial top Hessian eigenvalues/eigenvectors and correlate their concentration (fraction of trace in top-k) with trainability metrics (early-loss slope, steps-to-target, gradient-norm variance) for plain deep stacks, ResNet variants, and Transformer stacks to motivate targeted interventions. 3) Causal intervention experiments: under matched small parameter budgets (+0.5%-3%), compare (A) Hessian-aligned bypasses (align P to estimated top-k eigendirs and set initial gate to small damping value), (B) random low-rank bypasses (same param count but random directions), (C) naive long skips or added identity bypasses, and (D) optimizer-based preconditioners (KFAC-like short-run). Train on CIFAR-10/100 and Tiny-ImageNet and on a small LM task (WikiText-2) for Transformers. Metrics: steps-to-target, area under early-loss curve, final validation accuracy/perplexity, top Hessian eigenvalue evolution, loss-landscape sharpness (filter-normalized), and robustness to random weight perturbations and CIFAR-C. 4) Ablations & schedules: vary rank r, gate initialization and annealing schedules (early-only damping vs constant), frequency of eigenvector re-estimation (init-only, early-epoch re-estimates), and how to share eigenvectors across blocks (global vs per-stage). Test sensitivity and report defaults. 5) Mechanistic checks: in linearized toy networks compute exact Hessian eigendirs and show analytically how adding a small bypass aligned to top eigenvector reduces effective curvature experienced by gradient descent; replicate in synthetic nonlinear nets to confirm behavior. 6) Cost & robustness analysis: measure wall-clock overhead of eigenvector estimation and bypass modules, and quantify benefits per-added-parameter and per-unit compute compared to baselines. Evaluation metrics: Spearman/Pearson correlations, steps-to-target, final accuracy/perplexity, change in top Hessian eigenvalues, robustness under weight noise and corruptions, and compute/param overhead.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Noisy eigenvector estimates: early Hessian-vector probes are noisy and may misalign bypasses; mitigate by using few robust Lanczos iterations, averaging over small batches, using shared/global eigenvectors when per-block estimates are unstable, and re-estimating early epochs. 2) Interfering with expressivity: naive curvature cancellation could remove useful sharp features; use small initial gating/damping and anneal gates so bypasses act gently or only early. 3) Redundancy with optimizers: advanced second-order optimizers may already address curvature; we will compare and show per-parameter trade-offs and orthogonality benefits (architecture vs optimizer). 4) Computational overhead: Hessian-vector products and Lanczos add compute; keep probes infrequent (init and once early), use low iteration counts, and quantify overhead. 5) Domain dependence: Hessian concentration patterns vary by task/model, so benefits may be dataset/architecture dependent; experiments span CNNs and Transformers and include sensitivity analyses. 6) Theoretical difficulty: producing rigorous guarantees linking bypass alignment to convergence rates is challenging; we emphasize mechanistic linearized analyses and extensive empirical validation instead.

Name: activation_ricci_flow
Title: Activation Ricci Flow: Curvature-Guided Diagnostics and Early Rewiring for Trainable Deep Networks
Short Hypothesis: Time-varying, activation- and gradient-weighted discrete Ricci curvature on a model's block-level computation graph reveals structural information bottlenecks (over-squashing/over-stretching) that predict optimization difficulty and brittleness; cheaply approximating this curvature online and applying a curvature-flow inspired early rewiring (edge reweighting/gating or a tiny bypass added across negatively curved 'bridge' edges) reduces bottlenecks and improves convergence and robustness more efficiently than naive skip addition or non-geometric heuristics. This setting—block-level graphs with activation-derived neighborhood measures—is the right testbed because it captures dynamic, data-dependent connectivity that static topology measures miss while remaining tractable for academic compute.
Related Work: Discrete Ricci curvatures (Ollivier-Ricci, Forman-Ricci) have been applied to graphs for robustness, GNN design, pooling, and detecting critical edges; several recent papers use Ricci curvature for pruning or to enhance GNN message-passing. A 2021 study found Ollivier-Ricci curvature of architecture graphs correlates with robustness; 2026 preprints apply Ricci ideas to identify important NN edges via activation patterns. However, existing work either (a) computes curvature on static architecture graphs (structure-only), (b) applies curvature inside GNN message-passing design, or (c) uses curvature post-hoc for pruning. Our proposal is distinct by: (1) defining Ricci curvature on data-conditioned node measures derived from activations and gradients (activation-Ricci) so curvature is time-varying and task-aware, (2) developing scalable, low-cost approximations (Forman proxies, 1D-projection Wasserstein sketches, Sinkhorn with tiny support) to make online monitoring feasible, (3) introducing curvature-flow-inspired early rewiring that (greedily) reweights/gates or adds tiny bypasses specifically across negatively curved bridge edges to raise local curvature and alleviate information bottlenecks, and (4) conducting causal experiments across CNN and Transformer stacks comparing curvature-guided edits to baselines (random rewires, effective-resistance guided, Gramian-guided) with matched parameter budgets. These elements together make the proposal novel and not a trivial rehash of prior curvature applications.
Abstract: Why do some deep neural stacks train reliably while others with similar depth/parameters stall or become brittle? We propose a geometric, data-dependent structural diagnostic and intervention: compute a discrete Ricci curvature on a block-level computation graph where each node's neighborhood measure is derived from its activation/gradient distribution on small batches. Negative curvature identifies bridge-like bottlenecks where information (and gradient) transport is fragile or over-squashed; positive curvature indicates redundant, well-integrated regions. We (i) develop scalable estimators for activation-weighted Ollivier-Ricci curvature and Forman-Ricci proxies (random-projection Wasserstein sketches, low-support Sinkhorn, and combinatorial Forman approximations) that run cheaply at init and early epochs, (ii) show that early negative-curvature signatures predict slow convergence, exploding/vanishing gradient events, and sensitivity to weight perturbations across ResNet and Transformer families, and (iii) introduce curvature-flow-inspired early rewiring: a budgeted, greedy procedure that reweights edges, opens gated tiny bypasses, or inserts low-cost 1x1 bypass projections across the most negative-curvature edges to raise local curvature and broaden information pathways. We empirically compare curvature-guided interventions to resistance/min-cut/Gramian heuristics and random baselines on CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, and small language-modeling tasks, measuring steps-to-target, final accuracy/perplexity, gradient statistics, Hessian top eigenvalue, and robustness to weight and input perturbations. The result is a practical, geometric diagnostic and a small set of parameter-efficient, causal interventions for building more trainable and robust deep networks.
Experiments:
1) Definitions & scalable estimators:
   - Node graph: nodes = block outputs (residual block, transformer layer); edges = feedforward adjacency and explicit shortcuts.
   - Node measures: for node v use empirical distributions mu_v over minibatch pooled activations (global-pooled features or token-mean embeddings) and/or gradient magnitudes; compute pairwise Wasserstein-1 distances W1(mu_u, mu_v).
   - Curvature estimators: implement (A) activation-Ricci (Ollivier-Ricci) with W1 approximated by 1D-projection sketches (random projections of activations, median absolute deviation) and tiny Sinkhorn (support size 8-32); (B) Forman-Ricci combinatorial proxy using edge weights from activation similarities (cheap, no transport); and (C) hybrid smoothing (Forman to identify candidates then refine with ORC sketch).
   - Validate approximations against exact small-model ORC (ground-truth) to pick defaults minimizing compute.

2) Predictive correlation study: construct controlled families: plain deep stacks, ResNet variants (vary skip density/scale), Dense-like local skips, and Transformer encoder stacks. For each architecture compute early activation-Ricci and Forman summaries (per-edge curvature, curvature histogram, fraction negative, curvature bottleneck score) at init and after a few epochs. Correlate (Spearman, partial controlling for depth/params) with: steps-to-target loss/accuracy on CIFAR-10/100 and Tiny-ImageNet, early-loss slope, frequency of gradient explosion/vanishing, initial gradient-norm variance, top Hessian eigenvalue, and robustness to Gaussian weight perturbations and CIFAR-C. Report predictive power vs baseline metrics (effective resistance, min-cut, Gramian/Hankel, gradient-frustration).

3) Curvature-flow early rewiring (causal tests): design a budgeted procedure applied in early training (first T_e epochs): (A) rank edges by negative curvature and pick top-B bridge edges; (B) for each selected edge perform one of: reweight edge (multiply by small learnable scalar >1), add gated low-rank bypass (1x1 conv/projection) with gate initialized near 0 and then warmed up, or open an existing dormant skip. Use greedy selection to maximize estimated local curvature increase per added param. Compare to baselines: random-add, resistance-guided add, min-cut guided add, and adding same-budget random bypasses. Evaluate training speed, final accuracy, gradient stats, Hessian spectra, and robustness to weight/input noise. Track curvature evolution pre/post intervention.

4) Ablations and robustness: test Forman-only vs ORC-sketch pipelines; test sensitivity to batch size and projection count; vary B and T_e to map cost-benefit; test directed vs symmetrized graph encodings. Evaluate whether curvature-guided minimal edits outperform dense skip patterns (DenseNet) per parameter.

5) Mechanistic analyses: on linearized toy networks derive how negative ORC bridges correspond to narrow transport between distributions and to vanishing-backprop channels; simulate how adding bypass across negative edge increases gradient throughput analytically and empirically.

6) Cross-family study: apply to transformer encoder stacks on WikiText-2 and small GLUE tasks (use token-mean activation measures) to show generality across modalities.

Evaluation metrics: Spearman/Pearson correlations, steps-to-target, early-loss slope, final validation accuracy/perplexity, gradient-norm distributions, top Hessian eigenvalue, robustness under random weight perturbations and CIFAR-C, and extra compute overhead for curvature estimation. All experiments are sized to fit single multi-GPU academic resources by using block-level graphs and lightweight curvature approximations.
Risk Factors and Limitations:
1) Computational cost & noise: accurate ORC needs transport distances and can be expensive/noisy on high-dim activations. Mitigations: use Forman proxies as cheap prefilter, 1D-projection Wasserstein sketches, tiny Sinkhorn supports, small held minibatches, and infrequent early evaluations. We will report estimator error vs cost.
2) Confounding with size/width: curvature may correlate with width/parameters. We will control for depth and parameter count, use matched-budget interventions, and report partial correlations.
3) Causality: negative-curvature correlation with poor training may be non-causal. We include causal early-rewiring experiments and linearized toy proofs to test and explain causality.
4) Modeling choices: defining node measures (activations vs gradients, pooling choices) and graph directionality affect curvature. We will test multiple operationalizations and report sensitivity.
5) Limited scope: curvature-guided edits best fit modular stacks with clear block outputs (ResNets/Transformers); application to micro-NAS cells or highly recurrent models may require adaptation. 
6) Prior art overlap: earlier works used Ricci curvature for pruning and GNN design; our novelty rests on activation-weighted, time-varying curvature diagnostics and curvature-flow inspired early rewiring/edge reweighting with scalable estimators and causal tests. If effects are marginal, the contribution will still be a clear assessment of curvature's practical value and a usable low-cost pipeline for curvature-based diagnostics.

Name: interface_procrustes
Title: Interface Procrustes: Aligning Adjacent Layer Subspaces to Make Deep Networks Immediately Trainable
Short Hypothesis: A sizable fraction of early training is spent rotating and reorienting adjacent layer representations so they can communicate; this "interface misalignment" between consecutive block subspaces predicts optimization difficulty. Cheap, low-rank, data-driven interface transforms—initialized by a Procrustes-style alignment (or enforced briefly with a light alignment loss)—that map the principal subspace of a layer into the principal subspace of the next layer reduce wasted early updates, speed convergence, and improve robustness with minimal parameter overhead. Studying block-level interfaces (residual blocks, transformer layers) isolates structural alignment effects from other factors and is the right setting because skips make blocks explicit and small batch-based SVD/CCA estimates are tractable.
Related Work: Researchers have observed that deep networks spend much early optimization aligning internal bases (representation dynamics literature) and used tools like SVCCA/CKA to compare learned layer representations. Data-dependent initialization and orthonormal/whitening initializations (Saxe et al., dynamical isometry work) aim to improve signal propagation but focus on local singular-value properties rather than inter-layer subspace orientation. Recent work analyzing dynamics (e.g., Bordelon & Pehlevan, 2022) studies feature evolution but does not propose structural, local interface interventions. Low-rank adapters and LoRA add path-specific capacity but are not typically initialized to align adjacent subspaces. Our proposal differs in three ways: (1) we identify and quantify inter-layer misalignment at initialization/early training as a mechanistic bottleneck for gradient-based optimization; (2) we propose small, inexpensive Procrustes-initialized interface modules (and an optional short-lived alignment loss) that directly rotate/align principal subspaces between adjacent blocks rather than globally reshaping singular spectra; and (3) we provide causal tests across CNN and Transformer families showing that local alignment gives large per-parameter gains versus naive skip/adapters or global spectral interventions. This is not a trivial combination of prior ideas because the intervention targets local subspace geometry (orientation) rather than singular-value magnitudes or global propagation modes.
Abstract: Why do deep modular stacks with residuals or attention often still require many early steps of slow progress? We propose that a substantial part of early training cost is due to inter-layer interface misalignment: consecutive blocks learn bases independently and must spend SGD steps rotating these bases into mutual alignment so signals and gradients pass efficiently. We introduce Interface Procrustes, a simple, lightweight structural tool: estimate the top-k principal subspaces of adjacent block outputs (or their Jacobian images) on a small held minibatch and compute a low-rank orthogonal transform (Procrustes map) that best maps the source subspace to the target. Implement this as tiny, gated, low-rank interface adapters or as an early alignment loss that nudges blocks to match mapped subspaces. We hypothesize (i) initial inter-layer subspace angles predict early optimization difficulty across ResNet-like CNNs and Transformer stacks, and (ii) Procrustes-initialized adapters or brief alignment objectives materially accelerate convergence, reduce gradient variance, and improve perturbation robustness per added parameter compared to random adapters or global spectral fixes. The method uses randomized SVD/CCA for scalability, is applied at block granularity to keep node counts small, and is cheap (small rank r, infrequent estimates). We validate through correlation studies, targeted interventions (init-only and early-regularized), ablations (rank, timing, batch size), and cross-family tests on CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, and small LM tasks. The result is a practical, data-aware interface design principle that complements existing structural and spectral perspectives on trainability.
Experiments: 1) Measurement & Diagnostics: define nodes as block outputs (post-residual output or transformer-layer output). For a small held minibatch at initialization, compute top-k principal components for each block (randomized SVD, k in {8,16}). For each consecutive pair (b -> b+1) compute subspace principal angles, canonical correlations (CCA/CCA-sum), and Procrustes alignment error (||Y - X R||_F after optimal orthogonal R). Validate estimator stability vs batch size and k. Record these inter-layer alignment metrics across controlled architecture families (plain deep stacks, ResNet variants with different skip densities/scales, Transformer encoders with/without pre-LN). Correlate initial alignment measures with trainability outcomes (Spearman/Pearson, partial correlations controlling for depth/params): steps-to-target training loss/accuracy on CIFAR-10/100 & Tiny-ImageNet, early-loss slope, gradient-norm variance, and frequency of instability events.

2) Procrustes Initialization (init-only): implement per-interface low-rank adapters A_b that map block b outputs into block b+1 input space. Compute optimal low-rank orthogonal transform R_b via Procrustes on top-k PCs of block b and block b+1 using a small held batch; initialize A_b to approximate R_b restricted to rank r (r <= k, e.g., r in {4,8}). Make adapters gated by scalar g_b initialized small (or 1 depending on safe default). Train models with adapters vs baselines: (a) no adapters, (b) random low-rank adapters (same param budget), (c) PCA-initialized global low-rank skips, (d) spectral alignment baseline (align to data PCs globally). Measurement: steps-to-target, final accuracy/perplexity, gradient cosines with data subspaces, top Hessian eigenvalue, and robustness to random weight perturbations and CIFAR-C corruptions. Evaluate parameter-efficiency (gain per added param).

3) Early Alignment Loss (train-time nudging): instead of inserting adapters, add a lightweight alignment loss L_align = lambda * sum_b ||Proj_{V_{b+1}}(s_b) - Proj_{V_{b+1}}(R_b s_b)||^2 or simpler L = lambda * sum_b ||(I - P_{b+1}) R_b s_b||^2 computed on small minibatches, where R_b is the Procrustes map estimated at init and P_{b+1} projects onto top-k of block b+1. Apply this loss only during early epochs E (e.g., first 5-10 epochs) and then remove. Compare to (a) continuing adapters, (b) continuing alignment loss, and (c) other early regularizers like Gramian trace or LayerScale. Metrics as above plus compute overhead.

4) Dynamic Re-estimation & Adaptive Schemes: test variations where Procrustes maps are re-estimated after a few epochs (init-only vs re-estimate at epoch 5), and compare gating schedules (unfreeze gates after E epochs vs keep fixed). Investigate whether aligning Jacobian-image subspaces (using Jacobian-vector products to get J_x->s principal directions) is more predictive/useful than aligning raw activations for certain architectures (Transformers with token dependencies).

5) Synthetic controlled tests & linear theory: create toy deep linear stacks where each layer applies a random orthogonal rotation followed by a diagonal gain; analytically quantify how misaligned random rotations slow gradient descent (increase condition number of effective end-to-end matrix) and show how inserting the exact Procrustes rotations removes this overhead. Empirically verify on synthetic non-linear tasks where target features are aligned to specific subspaces to isolate mechanism.

6) Cross-family generality: replicate most effective interventions on Transformer encoder stacks (attention + FFN residuals) trained on small language modeling (WikiText-2 / PTB) and on MLP-based models on synthetic anisotropic data to show when interface alignment helps most. Also measure interaction with normalization choices (pre-LN vs post-LN) and stochastic depth.

7) Ablations and comparisons: vary rank r, batch size used for PCA, whether to use orthogonal Procrustes vs unconstrained linear least-squares transform, and test sensitivity to noisy estimates. Directly compare predictive and interventional power of interface alignment to prior metrics (effective resistance, Gramian/Hankel, gradient-frustration, min-cut), showing unique variance explained and parameter-efficiency.

Evaluation metrics: Spearman/Pearson correlations of initial alignment vs trainability, steps-to-target, early-loss slope, final accuracy/perplexity, gradient-norm variance, top Hessian eigenvalue, robustness under random weight perturbations and CIFAR-C, parameter-efficiency (accuracy gain per added parameter), and compute overhead (time per epoch) for estimator and adapters.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Nonstationarity of modes: subspaces at initialization may quickly change, reducing long-run benefit of an init-only Procrustes. Mitigation: test early re-estimation, apply adapters as gently gated and optionally anneal, and focus on early-training gains which are the main target.

2) Batch-sample bias: PCA/CCA computed on tiny held batches can mis-estimate principal directions and overfit to idiosyncrasies. Mitigation: use randomized SVD with small oversampling, aggregate a few micro-batches, and report sensitivity to batch size; prefer small r to reduce overfitting risk.

3) Interfering with expressivity: forcing alignment may reduce representational diversity and harm final performance. Mitigation: keep adapter ranks and gate strengths small, apply alignment only early (anneal away), and compare to baselines to detect harms.

4) Added parameters and overhead: adapters and occasional SVD/Lanczos steps add modest compute and params. We constrain adapter ranks to be tiny (r <= 8), run estimators infrequently (init and optionally once early), and provide wall-clock trade-offs. Expected overhead fits academic multi-GPU budgets.

5) Granularity choice sensitivity: defining nodes as entire residual blocks vs sub-blocks affects results. We will evaluate multiple granularities and recommend defaults (block-level for ResNets, layer-level for Transformers).

6) Limited scope: best suited to modular stacks with clear block outputs; highly entangled micro-NAS cells or recurrent models may need adaptation. If alignment has marginal effect on some families/datasets, we will analyze conditions (spectral gaps, anisotropy of data) where it matters.

Name: hebbian_rewiring_scaffold
Title: Hebbian Rewiring: Local Plasticity Rules for Activity-Driven Shortcut Formation in Deep Networks
Short Hypothesis: A tiny budget of shortcuts placed by cheap, local Hebbian-style plasticity rules (online pre–post activity covariance / STDP-like updates with homeostasis and decay) during an early plasticity window will produce larger per-parameter gains in optimization speed, stability, and robustness than random or globally-informed shortcut heuristics. Because the rule is entirely local (no backward gradients required) it is cheap, biologically plausible, and effective in block-structured stacks (ResNets, Transformer layers), making it the ideal setting to isolate structural wiring effects from optimizer magic or massive parameter changes.
Related Work: Prior work adds shortcuts or rewires based on global signals: Random/long skips, DenseNet, small-world rewiring, and activity- or gradient-based greedy rewiring (some SWANN-style and early activity-driven proposals). Dynamic sparse training (RigL) uses gradient magnitudes to rewire weights. Our approach differs in three key ways: (1) purely local plasticity: updates use only pre-/post-activation statistics (or simple temporal STDP variants), not gradient or global graph metrics; (2) online continuous plasticity during an early window with homeostatic normalization and decay to avoid runaway connectivity; (3) structural outcomes are cheap gated bypasses with tight param budgets and dynamics inspired by biological Hebbian/STDP rules rather than greedy offline selection. This is not a trivial rephrase of prior activity-based rewiring because local Hebbian dynamics have different inductive biases (favoring statistically coupled modules) and different costs/robustness properties than correlation-then-greedy selection.
Abstract: Shortcut connections (residuals, skips) dramatically improve deep network optimization, but where to place a few high-impact shortcuts remains an open design problem. We introduce Hebbian Rewiring, a lightweight, biologically inspired structural algorithm that forms and prunes a small set of gated bypasses using only local pre–post activity statistics computed online. During a short early plasticity window the model continuously tracks exponentially weighted pre/post pooled activations and updates per-candidate coupling scores with a Hebb-like rule (covariance or STDP temporal variant), combined with homeostatic normalization, decay, and a global sparsity/budget constraint. The top-scoring candidate edges are instantiated as tiny gated bypass modules (low-rank 1x1 projections when needed) whose gates are warmed up slowly. Hebbian Rewiring requires no backward gradients for decision-making, has O(B) extra state for a budget of B edges, and naturally discovers connector shortcuts linking correlated modules while avoiding trivial dense wiring via normalization and decay. We will (i) compare Hebbian Rewiring to random and global heuristics (resistance/min-cut/greedy activity) across ResNet and Transformer stacks on CIFAR/Tiny-ImageNet and small language-model tasks; (ii) analyze how the plasticity dynamics change graph statistics (clustering, algebraic connectivity) and gradient propagation; and (iii) study robustness, parameter-efficiency, and sensitivity to plasticity hyperparameters. The method yields a cheap, interpretable wiring rule and practical design guidelines for when and how to let networks self-organize useful shortcut structure.
Experiments:
1) Implementation & defaults: Define nodes as block outputs (post-residual, transformer layer output). For each candidate directed pair (i -> j) with j > i+1 within a maximum hop distance H, maintain an EMA of pooled activities a_i(t), b_j(t) and of cross-covariance C_ij(t) <- (1-\alpha) C_ij + \alpha (phi(a_i) \cdot psi(b_j)). Use simple scalar summaries by global-pooling or low-dim random projections to keep cost O(dim_proj). Update rule variants: (A) Hebb: delta C ~ a_i * b_j; (B) STDP-like: correlate temporally shifted activations for sequential tasks. Maintain per-node homeostatic normalization dividing by E[||a_i||^2] to avoid hub formation and apply exponential decay. Every R steps select top-B candidate edges by score (C_ij normalized) and instantiate gated bypasses (tiny projection if dims mismatch). Gates initialized closed (near zero) and warmed up with small learning rate; prune edges whose score decays below threshold. Provide concrete hyperdefaults: H=8, proj-dim=32, \alpha=0.01, B in {4,8,16}, R=epoch or few hundred steps.

2) Baselines & families: Compare to: (i) no-extra-skips (vanilla ResNet/Transformer), (ii) random-add, (iii) greedy activity-correlation (global ranking once), (iv) resistance/min-cut guided adds, and (v) modular small-world rewiring (as earlier proposal). Evaluate on ResNet-50-like stacks (CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet subset) and Transformer encoder stacks (WikiText-2 small LM) with matched parameter budgets.

3) Metrics & analyses: Primary: steps-to-target training loss, early-loss slope, final validation accuracy/perplexity. Secondary: gradient-norm statistics, top Hessian eigenvalue, algebraic connectivity/clustering of learned block graph, robustness to random weight perturbations and CIFAR-C/common corruptions, and edge stability (how persistent are Hebbian-formed shortcuts). Also report compute overhead and extra params.

4) Ablations: (A) local-only Hebb vs Hebb+gradient hybrid; (B) covariance vs STDP temporal variant; (C) different homeostasis (L1 vs L2 normalization); (D) plasticity window length (early-only vs continuous); (E) budget B sweeps to measure diminishing returns.

5) Mechanistic checks: toy linear deep networks where analytic pre/post covariance indicates useful bypasses; show how Hebbian-formed edges reduce effective resistance and increase gradient throughput. Visualize discovered edges and compare to greedy global picks to highlight qualitative differences.

6) Practical evaluation: report default hyperparameters that work robustly and per-parameter improvement curves (accuracy gain per extra param) to show parameter-efficiency.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1) Trivial identity-bridging: local covariance may prefer trivial identity-like shortcuts that short-circuit learning and reduce expressivity. Mitigations: homeostatic normalization, thresholding, warm gated opening, and early-only plasticity windows. 2) Spurious correlations: minibatch noise can create unstable or noisy scores. Mitigations: EMA smoothing, low-dim projections, and selecting edges only after score stabilization with a minimum significance test. 3) Redundancy with learned attention: in Transformers, learned attention may already route information; rewiring gains could be small. We will evaluate cross-family and report where Hebbian Rewiring helps most (likely deep CNN stages and modular stacks). 4) Hyperparameter sensitivity: decay rates, probe dim, budget B may need tuning; we include sensitivity sweeps and provide robust defaults. 5) Non-causality: correlation does not imply causal usefulness; we test causality by ablation (prune formed edges) and short-run intervention checks. 6) Applicability: best suited for block-structured networks; micro-NAS cells or models without clear intermediate pooling may require adaptation. 7) Theoretical limits: rigorous bounds connecting local Hebbian formation to global optimization rates are challenging; emphasis is empirical, mechanistic, and prescriptive rather than formal guarantees.
